🚨 Upozorňujeme na zranitelnosti v pgAdmin 4, CVE-2026-12046, CVE-2026-12045, CVE-2026-12048.   Tyto zranitelnosti zahrnují neautentizovanou deserializaci (pickle) v routách SQL Editoru (close a update_connection), která umožňuje vzdálené spuštění kódu, zranitelnost AI Assistant Prompt Injection kombinovanou se SQL Injection a obcházením read-only transakcí, a také uložený Cross-Site Scripting prostřednictvím nedůvěryhodného textu chyb a plan-node renderovaného přes html-react-parser. Útočník může za určitých podmínek bez autentizace spustit škodlivý kód, manipulovat s databázovými dotazy, obcházet bezpečnostní omezení a injektovat skripty vedoucí ke kompromitaci uživatelských relací nebo dat.   📌Doporučujeme aktualizovat na pgAdmin verze 9.16 nebo novější.

🛡️ Una falla en Splunk puede abrir la puerta al monitoreo de tu empresa  Splunk es una plataforma usada para centralizar logs, alertas y eventos de seguridad.  Muchas empresas lo usan para revisar qué está pasando en servidores, aplicaciones y redes.  Si Splunk queda expuesto, el atacante puede afectar justo la herramienta que ayuda a detectar incidentes.  CISA, agencia de ciberseguridad de Estados Unidos, alertó que la falla ya está siendo explotada.  Pidió corregirla con urgencia.  ⚠️ ¿Qué pasó?  Según BleepingComputer, la falla CVE-2026-20253 afecta Splunk Enterprise en versiones 10.2.0 a 10.2.3 y 10.0.0 a 10.0.6.  Una CVE es el número público que identifica una vulnerabilidad.  El problema está en un servicio lateral de PostgreSQL.  PostgreSQL es una base de datos usada por muchas aplicaciones.  La falla permite que un atacante remoto, sin usuario ni contraseña, cree o recorte archivos en sistemas vulnerables.  Investigadores advirtieron que también podría facilitar ejecución remota de código.  Eso significa mandar instrucciones al servidor desde fuera.  💡 ¿Qué deben hacer?  Pidan a TIC confirmar si usan Splunk Enterprise.  Revisen versiones 10.2.x y 10.0.x afectadas.  Apliquen el parche de Splunk de inmediato.  Si no pueden parchar hoy, desactiven temporalmente el servicio PostgreSQL sidecar.  Cierren Splunk a internet si no es indispensable.  Revisen logs por archivos modificados, accesos raros o actividad fuera de horario.

A meme payload was injected into a national trust system.That is the real story.The alien message is the hook. The deeper issue is that an emergency-alert channel exists to interrupt sleep, override attention, bypass normal phone behavior, and tell people what reality is during disasters. If that channel can be abused, the attacker is not just hacking software. They are hacking public trust, emergency compliance, and the state’s ability to warn people during floods, landslides, storms, and other genuine threats.Factual spine to tighten Use these points as the verified backbone.Brazil’s Ministry of Integration and Regional Development said the Defesa Civil Alerta platform was taken offline at 1:30 a.m. on Saturday, June 20, 2026, after an unauthorized remote activation sent an “Alerta Extremo” containing the word “misantropia”; the ministry said it was probably a hacker attack and said the Federal Police would be acionada/involved.Agência Brasil later reported that mobile users in at least seven states plus the Federal District received false messages between 11:41 p.m. Friday, June 19, and 1:23 a.m. Saturday, June 20. The affected places included Belo Horizonte, Brasília, Campo Grande, Curitiba, Rio Branco, Rio de Janeiro, Salvador, and São Paulo; those cities together have about 30 million people, and alerts also went to smaller municipalities in São Paulo, Rio de Janeiro, and Mato Grosso do Sul.The government said there were 10 different false notifications: nine via Cell Broadcast and one via SMS. Cell Broadcast matters because it can reach phones in a geographic area without people signing up first.One important nuance: the Belo Horizonte “tornado / alien attack” message was reported as arriving through WhatsApp from an official Civil Defense channel, not necessarily the same screen-freezing “Extreme Alert” path that sounded alarms in other states. O Tempo reported that the BH message said there was a “Possibility of Tornado” and then added “ATAQUE ALIENIGENA, HUMANOS CHEGAMOS…”; the outlet also noted that in Minas Gerais those alerts were not of the extreme/severe model that beeps and locks the phone screen.Later reporting from Folha and Poder360 said credentials linked to two Civil Defense agents in Pará were used to send the false alerts, and that those accounts were supposedly authorized only for Pará. Treat that as reported/under investigation, not final forensic truth, unless a final official report confirms it.Biggest improvement to your post Change the frame from: “Aliens did not invade Brazil.”to: “Aliens did not invade Brazil. Someone invaded Brazil’s ability to tell millions of people what is real in an emergency.”That is much stronger.The best line in your draft is already: “The aliens did not invade Brazil, someone invaded Brazil’s emergency alert system.”Upgrade it to: “The aliens did not invade Brazil. Someone invaded the country’s emergency trust layer.”That phrase — emergency trust layer — is the high-IQ frame.Stronger opening Your current opening is descriptive. Make it more cinematic: At around 1 a.m., phones across Brazil started screaming.Not for a flood. Not for a landslide. Not for a tornado.For an alien attack.One message reportedly sent to residents in Belo Horizonte warned of a tornado before adding:“Protect yourself: ALIEN ATTACK. Humans, we have arrived.”There were no aliens.But there was an invasion.That opening is much more viral because it creates suspense before revealing the real issue.Best thesis Use this: This was not a UFO story. It was a cyberattack on public reality infrastructure.Or even sharper: Emergency alert systems are not just notification systems. They are state-backed reality injection systems.That sounds intense, but it is accurate as a concept: these systems tell people what is happening and what to do when they may not have time to verify.The missing big idea The most important missing idea is trust decay.A fake alert can harm public safety even if nobody is physically injured that night, because it trains people to doubt future alerts.The true risk is not only: “People panicked over aliens.”The true risk is: “Next time there is a real flood, landslide, wildfire, tornado, or dam failure, some people may hesitate because the last alert sounded like a meme.”That is the serious second-order consequence.Add this line: The most dangerous payload was not “alien attack.” The most dangerous payload was doubt.Better post structure Use this structure:Hook: phones woke people up with bizarre alerts. Absurd detail: alien attack / misanthropy. Correction: no aliens, no disclosure, no apocalypse. Reality: unauthorized activation of Civil Defense warning infrastructure. Why it matters: emergency alert systems rely on instant trust. Security lesson: these systems need stronger governance than ordinary apps. Closing punchline: the invasion was not extraterrestrial; it was infrastructural. Polished rewritten version Here is a stronger version of the full post: “Humans, we have arrived.”That is not the start of a Netflix alien-invasion series.It is reportedly what some people in Brazil saw after false emergency alerts were sent through channels linked to the country’s Civil Defense warning system.In Belo Horizonte, one message reportedly warned of a possible tornado before adding:“Protect yourself: ALIEN ATTACK. Humans, we have arrived.”Elsewhere, people received “Extreme Alert” messages containing words like misantropia — misanthropy, or hatred/distrust of humanity.There was no alien invasion.But there was an invasion.Brazil’s government says its Defesa Civil Alerta platform was remotely activated by someone outside the national Civil Defense system. The platform was taken offline at around 1:30 a.m. on Saturday, June 20, and the Federal Police are investigating what officials believe was probably a cyberattack.That is the part that matters.The alien message is funny. The security failure is not.Emergency alerts are not normal notifications. They are designed to override attention, interrupt sleep, and tell people what to do during floods, storms, landslides, and other real threats.If attackers can inject nonsense into that channel, the damage is not just confusion.The damage is trust decay.Next time the alert is real, some people may hesitate.So yes, “alien attack” is absurd.But the real headline is darker:Someone did not hack Brazil with aliens. They hacked Brazil’s emergency trust layer.More dramatic version  Brazil did not get invaded by aliens.It got invaded by a push notification.In the middle of the night, emergency messages tied to Civil Defense channels reportedly warned people about “misantropia” and even an “ALIEN ATTACK.”Funny for about five seconds.Then terrifying.Because emergency alerts are one of the few technologies that can bypass normal attention and say:“Stop what you are doing. Reality has changed. Act now.”That power depends entirely on trust.If someone can hijack it for a joke, they can also hijack it for panic, evacuation chaos, market disruption, political manipulation, or disaster-response sabotage.The aliens did not arrive.But the future did:cyberattacks on public reality infrastructure.Punchier viral version  Brazil’s “alien attack” alert is funny until you realize what actually happened.The aliens did not invade.Someone allegedly accessed a national emergency-alert system and used it to send absurd messages to people’s phones in the middle of the night.That is not just a prank.That is an attack on emergency trust.These systems exist so people instantly obey when there is a flood, landslide, tornado, or other life-threatening event.If citizens start treating alerts like memes, the next real disaster gets more dangerous.The payload was “ALIEN ATTACK.”The target was public trust.Best one-liners Use these anywhere: The aliens did not invade Brazil. Someone invaded the siren. This was not a UFO incident. It was an unauthorized write-access event to public reality. The payload was funny. The target was trust. “Alien attack” was the joke. Emergency-alert integrity was the victim. This was not extraterrestrial disclosure. It was infrastructure disclosure. Emergency alerts are the state’s panic button. Someone else pressed it. A clown payload can still be a serious cyberattack. The attacker did not need malware on people’s phones. They used the government’s own alarm bell. This is what happens when a meme enters critical infrastructure. The scary part is not that people believed in aliens. The scary part is that the message came from a channel people are supposed to believe. The next cyber frontier is not stealing data. It is stealing authority. If you can spoof the warning system, you can manufacture reality for a few minutes. The real invasion was not from space. It was into the public nervous system.Obscure but powerful framing 1. “Public reality infrastructure” Emergency alert systems are part of what you could call public reality infrastructure.They answer: What is happening? Is it dangerous? Where? What should I do now?If that layer is compromised, society temporarily loses a trusted source of immediate reality.This is more interesting than “hacker prank.”2. “The siren privilege” Some systems have ordinary notification privileges.Emergency systems have siren privilege.They can wake people up, interrupt screens, override silence, create immediate fear, and trigger protective behavior. Brazil’s DCA documentation says extreme alerts can appear over phone content and sound even when the phone is on silent mode.The line: Any system with siren privilege needs nuclear-grade governance.3. “Cognitive critical infrastructure” Power grids, airports, hospitals, telecom systems, and water treatment plants are obvious critical infrastructure.Emergency alerts are cognitive critical infrastructure.They coordinate perception and behavior at population scale.The attack target was not just servers. It was the population’s immediate understanding of reality.4. “Cry-wolf cyberattack” This is a cry-wolf attack.The direct effect is confusion.The indirect effect is worse: reducing future compliance with legitimate warnings.A false emergency alert can create a trust scar.5. “Meme-shaped sabotage” The payload was absurd by design.“Alien attack” spreads faster than “unauthorized alert issued.” That means the attacker may have chosen a meme payload because it guaranteed virality.The serious line: The absurdity was not a side effect. It was the distribution mechanism.6. “Authority laundering” The attacker did not need credibility.The system supplied the credibility.That is the whole danger: The message was stupid, but the channel was authoritative.7. “Ontological shock as attack surface” An alien invasion message is funny because it briefly attacks your model of reality.At 1:30 a.m., half-asleep, with an official emergency alert on your phone, the brain has a tiny pause: “Wait… what?”That moment is the exploit.Missing nuance to add Your draft says “Residents across Brazil,” which is good for a headline, but for accuracy say: “People in multiple Brazilian states and major cities…”That avoids implying literally all of Brazil received the alert.You should also distinguish the channels: Some false messages went through Cell Broadcast/SMS emergency-alert paths, while the Belo Horizonte alien/tornado message was reported by local outlets as a WhatsApp alert from an official Civil Defense channel.That nuance makes your post more credible.Also, do not say “Brazil’s emergency alert system was hacked” too confidently unless you phrase it as: “officials say the platform suffered an invasion / possible hacker attack” “investigators are still determining whether this was credential misuse, platform compromise, or another access failure”Because later reporting points toward credentials of Pará Civil Defense agents being used, but the final forensic details are still under investigation.Better “what actually happened” paragraph Use this: According to Brazilian officials and local reporting, the incident appears to have involved unauthorized access to the Defesa Civil Alerta ecosystem, with false messages sent between late Friday night and early Saturday morning. The government says 10 false notifications were issued, mostly through Cell Broadcast, and later reporting suggests credentials linked to Civil Defense agents in Pará may have been used outside their authorized territory. The investigation is still ongoing, so the exact intrusion path matters: this may be less “Hollywood hack” and more “credentialed access failure with national-scale blast radius.”That is a very high-quality paragraph.Security lessons to include The strongest “solution” section: Emergency alert systems need different security assumptions from ordinary government portals.Because the cost of abuse is not just data loss. It is public panic, public distrust, and possible noncompliance during future disasters.What Brazil — and every country — should implement 1. Two-person rule for extreme alerts No single account should be able to issue an “Extreme Alert” to a large population.For high-severity messages, require: one originator one independent approver jurisdiction match event-type match geofence confirmation final blast-radius confirmationThink nuclear launch protocol, but for public panic.2. Jurisdiction hard limits If an account belongs to Pará, it should not be able to send alerts to São Paulo, Rio de Janeiro, Minas Gerais, Paraná, Acre, Bahia, Mato Grosso do Sul, or the Federal District.Not as a policy.As a hard technical impossibility.The line: Permissions should not be “please only use this in your region.” Permissions should be cryptographically and technically unable to leave the region.3. Blast-radius preview Before an alert is sent, the interface should say: You are about to send an EXTREME alert. Estimated devices affected: 8,400,000. Regions: São Paulo capital + surrounding municipalities. Alarm: yes. Silent-mode override: yes. Human action required: immediate protective action. Confirm with second operator.That would prevent accidental or malicious “oops, national-scale” events.4. Controlled vocabulary for emergency type An official emergency alert should not allow totally arbitrary free text in its most sensitive fields.Use structured fields: disaster category COBRADE code location severity recommended action expiration time issuing authorityFree-text instructions can exist, but weird content should trigger a review hold.Words like “alien,” “misantropia,” profanity, slurs, political messages, market panic terms, military conflict terms, “nuclear,” “evacuate all,” or “attack” should trigger automatic escalation unless paired with valid official incident codes.5. Content anomaly detection A model should score every proposed alert: Is this semantically compatible with a real Civil Defense warning? Does the event type match the affected geography? Does the text contain impossible claims? Does it contain offensive language? Does it contain political persuasion? Does it contain unexplained existential language? Does it contain nonstandard abbreviations or leetspeak?If the message says “misantropi4”, it should not go out as an emergency alert.6. Hardware-bound authentication Use phishing-resistant authentication: FIDO2 security keys device-bound passkeys privileged access workstations impossible-travel detection IP/device reputation checks session reauthentication before extreme alerts no SMS-based MFA for privileged alert operatorsAn emergency alert platform should treat every “send” action like a high-risk transaction.7. Just-in-time privileges Operators should not have standing permission to send major alerts 24/7.They should request temporary authority: region event type severity time window approving supervisor incident IDPrivileges expire automatically.8. Immutable alert ledger Every alert should write to an append-only audit log: who initiated who approved device used location/IP jurisdiction event code target area estimated recipients exact message timestamps edits approval chain revocation attemptsThis should be available to investigators and auditors immediately.9. Public verification code Every official alert could include a short ID: DCA-BH-7F29Citizens could verify it on an official status page, hotline, WhatsApp bot, SMS number, or app.The key is not to make people verify before acting in a real emergency. The key is to let media, first responders, and citizens quickly confirm if something seems suspicious.10. Emergency correction channel When a false alert goes out, the system needs a predefined correction protocol: This previous alert was false. There is no current Civil Defense emergency for your area. Follow official channels only. Investigation in progress.But this is tricky: if the alert platform itself is compromised, the correction may need to go through radio, TV, telecom banners, state government channels, municipal channels, and verified social accounts.11. Alert transparency dashboard The public should be able to see: active alerts recently expired alerts issuing authority affected region reason last update correction statusThat makes “official reality” auditable.12. Operator simulation mode Government operators should have a training environment that looks similar but is technically impossible to connect to production recipients.No “test” or “demo” alert should ever be one click away from real phones.13. Red-team drills The system should be red-teamed for: compromised credentials rogue insider jurisdiction bypass fake disaster message malicious evacuation notice political message injection mass panic language multilingual edge cases insider collusion vendor compromise telecom pathway failureDo not wait for the next meme attack.14. Trust-repair postmortem After the investigation, publish a public postmortem: what happened what did not happen number of alerts affected regions whether data was exposed whether telecom infrastructure was compromised what controls failed what controls worked what changes were made when the system returns who audited itTrust does not automatically come back after “service restored.”The best “genius level” phrase Use this: This was an integrity attack, not a confidentiality attack.Most people think “cyberattack” means stolen data.Here, the scary part is different: Attackers allegedly changed what the government said to the public.That is an integrity failure in an authoritative communications system.A beautiful paragraph: The old cyber nightmare was “they stole the database.”The new cyber nightmare is “they made the government say something false to millions of people at 1:30 a.m.”That is not a data breach.That is an authority breach.Add this missing comparison This belongs in the post: Emergency alert systems are like digital sirens.If someone hacks a website, you refresh.If someone hacks a siren, people run.That line makes the stakes instantly understandable.Better ending Your current ending is good, but this is stronger: The alien message will become the meme.The security lesson should not.Because the next fake alert may not be funny.It may say:evacuate shelter in place contaminated water dam failure active attack chemical leak tsunami nuclear incidentThe aliens were fake.The vulnerability was not.Best X/Twitter thread version 1/ At around 1 a.m., phones across Brazil started screaming with emergency alerts.Some messages warned of “misantropia.” One in Belo Horizonte reportedly warned of a tornado, then added:“ALIEN ATTACK. Humans, we have arrived.”There were no aliens.But there was an invasion.2/ Brazil’s government says the Defesa Civil Alerta platform was remotely activated by someone outside the national Civil Defense system.The platform was taken offline at around 1:30 a.m. on June 20, and the Federal Police are investigating what officials believe was probably a cyberattack.3/ Agência Brasil reported that false messages reached users in at least seven states plus the Federal District.The alerts were sent between 11:41 p.m. Friday and 1:23 a.m. Saturday, affecting major cities including Belo Horizonte, Brasília, Curitiba, Rio, Salvador, São Paulo, and others.4/ The funny part is obvious.The serious part is that emergency alerts are not normal notifications.They are designed to interrupt sleep, override attention, and tell people how to act during floods, storms, landslides, and other real threats.5/ That means this was not just a prank.It was an attack on trust.If people start treating official emergency alerts like memes, future real warnings become less effective.The most dangerous payload was not “alien attack.”It was doubt.6/ The old cyber nightmare was:“They stole the database.”The new cyber nightmare is:“They made the government say something false to millions of people at 1:30 a.m.”That is not just a data breach.That is an authority breach.7/ Emergency alert systems need stronger controls than ordinary apps:two-person approval, jurisdiction locks, blast-radius previews, hardware-bound authentication, anomaly detection, immutable audit logs, public verification codes, and fast correction channels.8/ The aliens did not invade Brazil.Someone invaded the siren.And the next fake alert may not be funny.More serious LinkedIn-style version  Brazil’s false “alien attack” emergency alert is easy to laugh at.But the incident points to a serious cybersecurity category: integrity attacks against public warning infrastructure.According to Brazilian authorities, the Defesa Civil Alerta platform was taken offline after unauthorized remote activation sent false alerts to multiple regions. Agência Brasil reported that 10 false notifications were issued, mostly through Cell Broadcast, and that affected cities together represent roughly 30 million people.The payload was absurd: “misantropia,” “ataque alienígena,” and “humanos chegamos.”But the target was not absurd.Emergency alert systems are designed to override normal attention. They exist so people act quickly during real disasters.If attackers can inject false messages into that channel, they do not merely create confusion. They degrade future trust.The lesson is clear:public warning systems require critical-infrastructure-grade controls:phishing-resistant authentication two-person approval for extreme alerts hard jurisdiction boundaries blast-radius previews structured message templates anomaly detection for impossible content immutable audit logs rapid public correction channels independent post-incident review The alien invasion was fake.The trust problem is real.Even more viral version  Brazil’s alien invasion alert is funny until you realize the attacker did not hack people’s phones.They allegedly hacked the thing people are supposed to trust when their phones scream in the middle of the night.That is much worse.A fake alien alert is a meme.A fake evacuation alert is chaos.A fake dam-collapse alert is mass panic.A fake “do not evacuate” alert during a real disaster is lethal.Emergency alerts are not notifications.They are digital sirens.And someone else pressed the siren.Missing “policy proposal” section Add something like this if you want the post to become more than commentary: Every country with Cell Broadcast-style emergency alerts should require a Public Alert Integrity Standard:no single-user extreme alerts hard regional permission boundaries phishing-resistant MFA immutable public/private alert logs automated anomaly detection structured alert templates blast-radius confirmation emergency revocation protocol public verification endpoint mandatory post-incident disclosure Because if a system can wake up millions of people, it should not be secured like an ordinary dashboard.Best cybersecurity angle The phrase to use: This is a “write-access to reality” problem.A compromised emergency-alert system gives the attacker temporary write-access to the public’s perception of danger.That sounds abstract, but it is exactly what happened.People saw an official-looking alert and had to decide: Is this real? Am I safe? What do I do? Can I trust this channel?The attacker forced that decision at population scale.Add this “future threat” paragraph

Telegram fueled 152 incidents, acting as the primary hub for threat actor amplification and DDoS coordination.   Government and public sectors faced the heaviest burden with 107 targeted attacks. Morocco, Ghana, and Nigeria emerged as the most affected nations.   Read our latest Threat Intelligence Report to analyze the data and safeguard your infrastructure. Some threats never trigger an alert.  Our updated  Cyber Threat Hunting training helps SOC teams unc

sample_tweets.text: Google DeepMind is now treating advanced AI agents as potential insider threats, applying zero‑trust principles originally designed for rogue employees.   The new TRAIT&R framework mirrors the MITRE ATT&CK model and is already being tested against a million real coding‑agent tasks. Our updated  Cyber Threat Hunting training helps SOC teams uncover hidden attacker activity through practical labs and real-world investigations.  🎯 30 new lessons 🎯 MITRE ATT&CK methodologies 🎯 Windows, Linux & network hunting  Learn more: kas.pr/6yrq #ThreatHunting #CyberSecurity

DeepMind just published their AI Control Roadmap. It's the most significant industry validation of what we've been saying at AgentVet.  The framework treats AI agents as "insider threats." Not external attackers, but entities that already have access and might go off track. They built their threat modeling on MITRE ATT&CK, the same standard used for traditional cybersecurity. They analyzed a million coding agent trajectories. And their key finding: most flagged incidents aren't adversarial. They come from agent misinterpretation and overeagerness.  This is exactly the thesis AgentVet was built on. Model alignment isn't enough. You need system-level controls. You need monitoring. You need to treat the agent like a powerful employee who might be well-intentioned but still causes damage. DeepMind calls it "defense-in-depth." We call it the seven-component harness. Same conclusion from different directions.  DeepMind also published a separate policy paper: "Three Layers of Agent Security" covering individual agents, multi-agent systems, and the broader ecosystem. That's the same surface area AgentVet covers with scanning, monitoring, and certification.  The difference: DeepMind wrote the framework. AgentVet builds the product. Frameworks tell you what should exist. AgentVet tells you whether your specific agent actually passes.  Google's own security team concluded that you can't trust alignment alone. You need independent verification, continuous monitoring, and response protocols. That's the gap AgentVet fills. For companies that don't have DeepMind's resources but need the same level of assurance.  The market signal is clear. The frameworks are landing. The regulations are coming (EU AI Act, August 2). The question isn't whether agent security becomes a requirement. It's who you trust to verify yours.  deepmind.google/blog/securin…

🎯 Threat Hunting - Stop Waiting for Alerts. Start Looking for Attackers.  Reactive security is not enough.  Threat hunting is the proactive practice of searching through your environment for attackers who have already bypassed your defences. They are already inside. They are just quiet enough that your alerts have not fired yet.  Threat hunting is the discipline that finds them before they detonate.  What Is Threat Hunting?  Threat hunting is hypothesis-driven investigation. Rather than waiting for an alert, a threat hunter forms a hypothesis based on threat intelligence, attacker TTPs (Tactics, Techniques, and Procedures), or anomalous data - and then goes looking for evidence of that hypothesis in the environment.  It is the difference between: → Reactive: "An alert fired. Let me investigate." → Proactive: "Based on current threat intel, attackers targeting our sector use this technique. Let me look for it now."  The Threat Hunting Process:  Step 1 - Form a Hypothesis Start with a question. Examples: "Are any hosts in our environment beaconing to external IPs on unusual ports?" "Is there any evidence of living-off-the-land binaries being used for lateral movement?" "Are there any accounts accessing systems they have never touched before?"  Step 2 - Collect and Analyse Data Pull the relevant data from your SIEM, EDR, network flows, proxy logs, or DNS logs. Filter, search, and look for anomalies.  Step 3 - Investigate Leads Follow the data. Pivot from indicators to related events. Build a timeline. Determine if your hypothesis is confirmed, denied, or needs refinement.  Step 4 - Respond or Document If you find something, trigger incident response. If you find nothing, document your methodology and findings - negative hunts are still valuable because they improve your baseline and detection coverage.  Step 5 - Improve Detections Every successful hunt should result in a new or improved detection rule so that next time, the SIEM catches it automatically.  Key Threat Hunting Techniques:  ✅ IOC-Based Hunting - Searching for known Indicators of Compromise: malicious IPs, domains, file hashes, or registry keys from threat intelligence feeds.  ✅ TTP-Based Hunting - Using the MITRE ATT&CK framework to hunt for specific attacker behaviours rather than static indicators. Far more durable because TTPs change slowly.  ✅ Anomaly-Based Hunting - Looking for statistical deviations from baseline. Users logging in at unusual hours, hosts generating abnormal DNS traffic, processes spawning unexpected child processes.  ✅ Stack Counting - Counting the frequency of values in a dataset to identify rare outliers. Rare parent-child process relationships, unusual User-Agent strings, infrequent registry modifications.  Essential Data Sources for Threat Hunting:  → Windows Event Logs (4624, 4625, 4688, 4768, 4769, 7045) → Sysmon logs for rich process, network, and file telemetry → EDR telemetry (CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, Microsoft Defender) → Network flow data (NetFlow, Zeek/Bro) → DNS query logs → Proxy and web gateway logs → PowerShell script block logging  MITRE ATT&CK - Your Hunting Framework:  The MITRE ATT&CK framework is the threat hunter's bible. It maps real-world attacker behaviours into a structured matrix of tactics and techniques. Use it to:  → Prioritise which TTPs to hunt for based on threat actor groups targeting your sector → Identify gaps in your detection coverage → Build hunting hypotheses from specific techniques  The bottom line:  Threat hunting is what separates a passive SOC from a proactive one. Alerts tell you what your tools already know how to detect. Hunting finds the gaps - the threats that live in the silence between your rules.  Hunt before they strike.  #ThreatHunting #SOCAnalyst #BlueTeam #CyberSecurity #MITREATTACK #ThreatIntelligence #InfoSec #ProactiveSecurity

📌 754 structured cybersecurity skills for AI agents · Mapped to 5 frameworks: MITRE ATT&CK, NIST CSF 2.0, MITRE ATLAS, D3FEND & NIST AI RMF · agentskills.io standard · Works with Claude Code, GitHub Cop...  🔗 github.com/mukul975/Anthropi… #Python

Detection as Code in practice: a full CI/CD pipeline for Sigma rules cuts out the "edit and save in the SIEM console" failure mode with automated validation, translation, testing, and shadow-mode deployment.  Key details:  - The four-stage pipeline is validate, translate, test, deploy. sigma-cli (wrapping pySigma) handles validation and conversion with no paid tooling. The SigmaHQ/sigma-rules-validator GitHub Action checks rules against the official JSON Schema. A 30-line Python script enforces metadata completeness: title, status, date, author, logsource, detection, and MITRE ATT&CK tags are all required or CI fails.  - Translated outputs (Sentinel KQL, Splunk SPL, Elastic Lucene) live under platform-translations/ as build artifacts generated by CI, never hand-edited. Field mapping pipelines applied at conversion time are where silent failures hide: a missing pipeline flag produces a syntactically valid query that matches nothing.  - Every rule needs two JSON fixtures: one true-positive event and one true-negative. pytest evaluates compiled queries against both. CERT-EU's droid goes further, running actual Atomic Red Team simulations in a sandbox and verifying the Sigma rule fires on real telemetry, eliminating "the fixture was wrong, not the rule" failures.  - New rules deploy to the SIEM with alerting suppressed for at least seven days (shadow mode). Match volume and event samples get human review before promotion.  #DFIR_Radar

🔑 Privileged Access Management (PAM) - Why It Is the Most Critical Control in Your Security Stack  Most breaches do not start with zero-days.  They start with privileged accounts.  Admin credentials, service accounts, root access - these are the keys to your kingdom. And attackers know it. PAM is the discipline of controlling, monitoring, and auditing who can access these accounts, when, and why.  What Is PAM?  PAM is a cybersecurity strategy that protects accounts with elevated permissions - the ones that can install software, modify system configs, access sensitive databases, or change security settings. If an attacker gets hold of one of these accounts, it is game over.  Core PAM Concepts Every SOC Analyst Must Know:  ✅ Just-In-Time (JIT) Access - Privileged access is granted only when needed and automatically revoked after a set time window. No standing privileges sitting open 24/7 for attackers to exploit.  ✅ Least Privilege Principle - Users and systems get the minimum access required to do their job. Nothing more. This limits the blast radius if an account is compromised.  ✅ Privileged Session Management (PSM) - Every privileged session is recorded and monitored in real time. Full audit trail of what commands were run, what files were accessed, and what changes were made.  ✅ Password Vaulting - Privileged credentials are stored in an encrypted vault. Users never see the actual password - the PAM tool injects it automatically. Rotated regularly and automatically.  ✅ Multi-Factor Authentication for Privileged Accounts - Even with valid credentials, attackers cannot access privileged accounts without passing the additional authentication layer.  Why PAM Matters for SOC Analysts:  When you are investigating an incident, privileged account activity is always one of the first things you check. Attackers love to:  → Dump credentials and escalate privileges (MITRE ATT&CK T1078) → Use legitimate admin tools to blend in (Living off the Land) → Create backdoor admin accounts for persistence → Abuse service accounts that never get audited  PAM gives you visibility into all of this. Without it, you are flying blind.  Key Detections to Build Around PAM:  ⚠️ Alert on privileged logins outside business hours ⚠️ Alert on new admin account creation ⚠️ Alert on privilege escalation attempts ⚠️ Alert on lateral movement using admin shares or RDP ⚠️ Alert when a service account logs in interactively (it should never do this)  Top PAM Tools in the Industry:  → CyberArk - Industry leader, enterprise-grade PAM → BeyondTrust - Strong for remote access and endpoint privilege management → Delinea (formerly Thycotic) - Flexible and widely used → HashiCorp Vault - Developer-friendly secrets management → Microsoft PIM (Privileged Identity Management) - Built into Azure AD for cloud environments  The bottom line:  You cannot protect what you cannot see. PAM gives you control over the most dangerous accounts in your environment and the audit logs you need to detect and respond when something goes wrong.  Lock down your privileged access. It is not optional.  #PAM #PrivilegedAccess #CyberSecurity #SOCAnalyst #BlueTeam #IdentitySecurity #ZeroTrust #InfoSec

NATIONAL ASSOCIATION OF NIGERIAN STUDENTS (NANS)  22 June 2026  PRESS STATEMENT  NANS CONDEMNS ALLEGED NELFUND FRAUD AT D.S. ADEGBENRO ICT POLYTECHNIC, CALLS FOR EFCC INVESTIGATION AND IMMEDIATE REFUND OF STUDENTS' FUNDS  The National Association of Nigerian Students (NANS), the apex representative body of Nigerian students, expresses its deep concern and outrage over the disturbing allegations surrounding the disbursement and utilization of funds under the Nigerian Education Loan Fund (NELFUND) scheme at D.S. Adegbenro ICT Polytechnic, Itori, Ogun State.  My attention has been drawn to disturbing documents and reports clearly showing that while a sum of ₦355,000 was reportedly disbursed by NELFUND to a student, the institution's invoice and financial records reflect a significantly lower amount of ₦236,000 on the student's invoice, as against the amount available on the NELFUND portal.  This glaring discrepancy raises serious questions regarding accountability, transparency, and the management of funds intended to support Nigerian students by the management of D.S. Adegbenro ICT Polytechnic.  To be clear, NELFUND was established to ease the financial burden on students and expand access to education. Any attempt by individuals or the management of any institution to exploit the scheme for personal gain or divert funds meant for students is a betrayal of public trust and an attack on the educational aspirations of Nigerian youths.  Consequently, NANS hereby:  1. Condemns in the strongest possible terms any alleged diversion, misappropriation, inflation, or fraudulent disbursement of NELFUND funds by the management of D.S. Adegbenro ICT Polytechnic or any other institution found culpable.  2. Calls on the Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC) to immediately commence a comprehensive investigation into the circumstances surrounding the alleged discrepancy and the alleged diversion and misappropriation of NELFUND funds disbursed to D.S. Adegbenro ICT Polytechnic.  3. Urges the management of D.S. Adegbenro ICT Polytechnic to publicly clarify the discrepancy, provide a transparent account of all NELFUND-related transactions affecting its students, and make refunds where necessary.  4. Calls on NELFUND management to strengthen its monitoring and accountability mechanisms to prevent abuse of the scheme and ensure that all disbursements are utilized strictly for their intended purposes.  Let me state without any form of equivocation that under my leadership, NANS shall not compromise. As a body, we remain committed to protecting the interests of Nigerian students. The future of Nigerian students must not be compromised by acts of corruption, recklessness, or financial misconduct.  Signed:  Comr. Akinteye Babatunde Afeez, GCNS President, National Association of Nigerian Students (NANS)

Calling Qilin "just a criminal gang" is the same category error that took years to correct for North Korea's Lazarus Group.  Check Point attributes May's ransomware surge to a handful of criminal groups, including Qilin, with no state or geopolitical framing.  The contradiction sits in the framing itself: "purely criminal" versus the operational pattern Qilin actually follows. Independent assessments consistently describe Qilin as a Russian-speaking operation, and the group enforces a documented policy of excluding CIS-country targets — a trait widely associated with Kremlin-tolerated cybercrime ecosystems. Japanese commentary on the Asahi case has already raised this question in print, explicitly noting no conclusive evidence ties Qilin to the Kremlin, while still flagging the exemption pattern as worth watching given Russia's wartime financing pressures.  That's an inference, not a confirmed fact — and it should stay labeled as one. But the same inference, once confirmed for Lazarus, reshaped how governments fund cyber defense and impose sanctions. Treating every Russian-speaking RaaS operation as apolitical crime, when the exemption pattern says otherwise, is a policy choice, not a neutral default. A realistic alternative: sanction the safe-harbor jurisdictions and hosting infrastructure these groups depend on, the way Lazarus's financing channels eventually were.  prtimes.jp/main/html/rd/p/00…

If you lived at that time, would you have discerned that Jesus was Messiah?  I mean before His execution.  Or would you have believed the leadership accusing him of blasphemy, sabbath-breaking, consorting with lowlifes and Samaritans, and engaging in witchcraft?  He was Jesus of NAZARETH, but Messiah was supposed to come from the city of David, Bethlehem.  His mother conceived him out of wedlock. He probably looked very different from Joseph, meaning that his mother was a... you know.  In his entourage were a trained assassin, a TAX COLLECTOR, a bunch of ignorant fishermen, and women. "Service" women.  He was thrown out of his own synagogue for blasphemy. He encouraged his followers to break Shabbat by harvesting grain along the wayside. He defied the Sanhedrin and excoriated wise and learned men, calling them "whited sepulchres," full of corruption.  He refused to punish an adulteress. He claimed to be the Son of God — the Way, the Truth, and the Life. He claimed to fulfill the Law! he claimed to BE the law! He claimed to be the Lawgiver Himself!  He engaged in necromancy by raising Lazarus.  Do you see how everything that is familiar to us now was scandalous back then? Do you see how ordinary people might have heard such awful accusations about Him from people they trusted that they wouldn't have even LISTENED to Him?  Do you see how everything that is familiar to us now was scandalous back then? Do you see how ordinary people might have heard such awful accusations about Him from people they trusted that they wouldn't have even LISTENED to Him?  Imagine what people said about Christians while Rome tried to exterminate them! They would have said "These people follow a dead charlatan who blasphemed his way into execution, and then his followers stole his body from the tomb and claim he was resurrected like some god!"  If nasty, false, disgusting things can be said — and widely believed — of the Son of God, a perfect, sinless man, how much easier is it to smear a mere mortal like Joseph Smith?  Neo-religions were popping up like mushrooms during the early 1800s in NE U.S. Some were even weirder — and explicitly kinkier — than the "Mormons."   Those groups might have been mocked or denounced, but they weren't persecuted to the degree my ancestors were. Not even close. Early church members were chased out of upstate NY even though there was no polygamy even spoken about let alone practiced.   Then they were chased out of Ohio at gunpoint, then Missouri, then Illinois. Even after decamping to the middle of nowhere, the State still came after us.  That's WAY over the top. Way out of proportion to what my ancestors were doing and saying.  Look, if God establishes a Church, the Adversary is going to stir people up to murderous rage against it. Lies and distortions and slanders will surround it.  You can definitely argue that persecution doesn't prove divine origin, but at the same time it doesn't rule it out. On the contrary, it's a positive sign.  Especially given that we are highly productive, pro-social, law-abiding, service-oriented, family centered, trustworthy people.   "Oh, but that's to hide the demonic core."  Really? Good fruit coming from a corrupt tree?  I should think that if you find a much-slandered group that is actually well-behaved, you should pay attention.  Maybe you can discern divine influence by seeing whom the devil hates most.

Stuxnet was first publicly identified in 2010, but it had already been running inside Iran's Natanz enrichment facility for roughly two to three years before anyone outside the operation knew it existed. That gap matters. The weapon worked in silence, and the silence was part of the design.  The technical architecture is worth understanding in full, because the doctrine followed from it. Stuxnet targeted Siemens programmable logic controllers governing Iranian centrifuges, issuing commands that caused the centrifuges to physically destroy themselves while simultaneously feeding operators false readings showing normal performance. The machines were tearing themselves apart while the instruments said everything was fine. That combination, physical destruction plus sensor spoofing, was not an accident of engineering. It was a strategic choice about how to maximize damage while minimizing the window for response. Four separate zero-day vulnerabilities. Self-replicating propagation. Precision targeting logic designed to contain collateral spread. Industrial control system-specific payload. No one had built anything like it before, at least not anything that became publicly known.  The United States and Israel developed it jointly. That has never been formally acknowledged by either government, but it has also never been seriously disputed. The New York Times reporting in 2012, drawing on sources with direct knowledge, established the outline. NPR's June 2026 analysis treats it as established baseline, which it is.  What the NPR piece is doing, sixteen years after public discovery, is asking what Stuxnet actually built. The answer is uncomfortable.  The proof of concept Stuxnet demonstrated was this: a nation-state could cause physical destruction to an adversary's strategic infrastructure using only software, with plausible deniability, without a single soldier crossing a border, without a missile or a bomb, and with effects precise enough to avoid the kind of obvious provocation that triggers a conventional military response. That combination had never existed before. Once it was demonstrated, the logic was available to everyone.  Iran's response is the clearest direct line. After Natanz, Iran invested heavily in offensive cyber capabilities. This is documented. The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps Cyber Command grew substantially. Iranian APT groups, variously designated by researchers as APT33, APT34, APT35, and others, developed capabilities across espionage, disruption, and destructive attack. The Shamoon malware that hit Saudi Aramco in 2012, destroying data on roughly 30,000 workstations, came roughly two years after Stuxnet's public exposure. Iranian attribution to Shamoon has been widely assessed, though Tehran denied it. The timing and the context are not coincidental.  Russia had its own trajectory, but the Stuxnet precedent validated a doctrine Russian military planners had been developing conceptually. The attacks on Ukrainian power infrastructure in December 2015 and December 2016, using BlackEnergy and Industroyer respectively, were the operational expression of a Stuxnet-derived logic applied to a different target set. Lights went out in parts of Ukraine. The attackers had persistent access to industrial control systems. The operations were calibrated to cause disruption without triggering a full military response from NATO. That calibration, that space between demonstrable harm and the threshold of conventional war, is exactly the space Stuxnet proved existed.  China's approach has been somewhat different in emphasis. Chinese APT groups, including the cluster now tracked as Volt Typhoon, have been documented engaging in what U.S. intelligence has described as pre-positioning: achieving persistent access to American critical infrastructure including water systems, power grids, and communications networks, not for immediate exploitation but for potential wartime activation or coercive leverage. The CISA and NSA advisory on Volt Typhoon issued in 2024 was unusually direct about the assessed intent. This is not espionage infrastructure. It is sabotage infrastructure held in reserve. The concept of holding that kind of access, positioning it quietly for later use, descends directly from the Stuxnet model.  North Korea's cyber program is more financially motivated in its primary focus, but it has demonstrated destructive capability when directed. The Sony Pictures attack in 2014. WannaCry in 2017, which the U.S. UK, and others formally attributed to the Lazarus Group in 2018 and which caused somewhere between four and eight billion dollars in global damage. The destructive capacity is real and it has been used.  The proliferation dynamic NPR is documenting in 2026 is this: a capability that took extraordinary resources to develop in 2007 is now within reach of any state with a serious cyber investment and a few years of sustained effort. The technical barrier has dropped. The doctrine has spread. And the infrastructure for what the analysis calls "pre-positioned deterrence" now exists across multiple competing powers simultaneously.  The original rationale for Stuxnet, as best as can be reconstructed from reporting, was to buy time. The Natanz centrifuges were enriching uranium. The alternative being weighed was an Israeli airstrike, with all the escalatory risk that carried. A covert operation that set back the program by months or years without triggering a war looked like the better option. NPR frames it as a weapon that may have prevented a conventional military strike. That framing is defensible.  The cost of that choice is what the June 2026 analysis is actually about. The same logic that made Stuxnet attractive, deniable, precise, below the threshold of open war, made it a template that adversaries would inevitably adopt and adapt. Every state actor with serious capabilities now holds some version of this in reserve. The targets include power grids, water treatment, financial systems, communications infrastructure. The access has in many documented cases already been achieved. What has not happened yet, in most cases, is the decision to activate it.  That is the world Stuxnet built. Not a world where one country's centrifuges spun out of control in 2008. A world where multiple major powers have pre-positioned destructive access inside each other's critical systems and are making continuous calculations about whether the conditions for use have arrived.  The deterrence logic here is genuinely unstable in ways that nuclear deterrence, for all its horrors, is not. Nuclear weapons require physical delivery. The infrastructure for cyber pre-positioning is already inside the target. The action required to activate it is a software command. The attribution window can be narrow or disputed. The escalation ladder is not well-defined. There is no established treaty framework, no inspections regime, no red phone dedicated to this category of conflict. There have been bilateral cyber discussions between the U.S. and China, and there are norms conversations happening in various multilateral forums, but nothing binding, nothing with teeth.  Sixteen years from public discovery, Stuxnet's most durable legacy is not the centrifuges it destroyed. Those were eventually rebuilt. The legacy is the doctrine it proved, and the infrastructure that doctrine has since generated across every major state actor in the system.  foreigninterference.org/post… #foreigninterference #MalwareWarfare #IndustrialSabotage #ZeroDayExploitation #CriticalInfrastructureMapping #InfrastructureAttacks #CyberCapabilityDevelopment

This isn't random hackers anymore — it's a nation-state operation. 🚩

2026년 6월 21일(토) 암호화폐 시황 한눈에 보기 비트코인은 6월 19일부터 21일까지 약 6만 3천 달러에서 6만 4천 달러 박스권에서 횡보하고 있다. 6월 17일 연방준비제도(연준)의 금리 동결과 추가 인상 시그널, 그리고 사상 최대 규모인 30일 기준 63억 5천만 달러의 현물 ETF 순유출에도 불구하고 200주 이동평균선인 6만 2천 달러를 지켜내는 흐름이다. 시장은 위험회피 심리와 미국-이란 평화협정 기대가 정면으로 부딪치는 전환 국면에 놓여 있다. HYPE(하이퍼리퀴드)는 6월 16일 약 76.7달러로 사상 최고가를 경신한 뒤 현재 69~70달러대로 약 10% 조정받았다. 하이퍼이브이엠(HyperEVM), HIP-3, 키네틱(Kinetiq) 런치 등 생태계 모멘텀과 현물 ETF 자금 유입이 강세를 떠받치고 있으나, 7월 6일 예정된 코어 기여자 물량 해제(언락)가 단기 부담 요인이다. 시장은 여전히 '비트코인 시즌'에 머물러 있다. 비트코인 도미넌스가 약 58%, 알트코인 시즌 지수가 약 37, 공포탐욕지수가 극단적 공포 영역에 위치하면서, 알트코인 전반의 순환매보다는 인공지능·실물자산(RWA)·내러티브 중심의 선별적 강세가 시장을 지배하고 있다. 핵심 요약 가격 측면을 보면 비트코인은 6월 21일 기준 약 6만 4천 달러에서 6만 4,200달러 사이, 24시간 변동률은 0.5~0.9%, 주간으로는 거의 보합이다. 6월 19일 한때 6만 2,200달러까지 밀렸다가 반등했다. 주요 지지선은 200주 이동평균선이 위치한 6만 2천 달러, 저항선은 6만 5천에서 6만 6천 달러 구간이다. 현재가는 2025년 10월 6일 기록한 사상 최고가 12만 6천 달러 대비 약 49% 낮은 수준이다. 이더리움은 약 1,720~1,730달러로 24시간 보합, 주간 약 3% 상승했고 이더리움/비트코인 비율 약세가 이어지고 있다. 글램스테르담(Glamsterdam) 업그레이드는 3분기를 목표로 한다. 솔라나는 약 73~74달러로 24시간 2~4% 상승하며 시가총액 7위를 유지 중이고 모건스탠리가 솔라나 ETF를 신청했다. 리플(XRP)은 약 1.14달러, 바이낸스코인(BNB)은 약 588달러다. 시장 지표로는 비트코인 도미넌스 약 58%, 알트코인 시즌 지수 약 37, 공포탐욕지수는 6월 8일 8에서 6월 15일 20으로 소폭 회복했으나 여전히 극단적 공포 구간이다. HYPE와 워치리스트 측면에서 HYPE는 6월 16일 약 76.7달러로 사상 최고가를 찍은 뒤 현재 69~70달러대, 시가총액 약 152억 달러로 10위권이다. HIP-3 빌더 배포형 무기한 선물 미결제약정이 14억 달러를 돌파했고 키네틱 런치는 6월 11일 메인넷에 출시됐다. 벨벳(VELVET)은 약 0.43달러로 6월 12일 고점 대비 약 75% 하락했고, 사하라(SAHARA)는 약 0.013달러로 고점 대비 약 92% 하락하며 6월 19일 사상 최저를 기록했다. 비트웨이(BTW)는 비트코인 기반 레이어1 토큰으로 약 0.08달러, 24시간 66% 급등했으나 유통량 대비 완전희석가치(FDV)가 약 8억 달러로 전형적인 저유통·고FDV 구조다. 잰션(JCT)은 BNB체인 인공지능 레이어2로 약 0.0043달러, 시가총액 약 4,900만 달러다. 거시·지정학 측면에서 미국-이란 갈등은 2월 28일 개전, 4월 8일 휴전을 거쳐 6월 15일 양해각서(MOU) 디지털 서명, 6월 19일 스위스 정식 서명이 예정됐다. 호르무즈 해협은 원칙적으로 재개통됐으나 6월 20~21일 이란의 재봉쇄 위협으로 불확실성이 다시 불거졌다. 연준은 6월 17일 금리를 3.50~3.75%로 동결했고, 워시 신임 의장 취임 첫 회의에서 점도표 연말 중앙값을 3.8%로 올리며 인상 시그널을 냈다. 5월 소비자물가지수는 4.2%로 3년 최고치였고 달러 인덱스는 1년 최고치, 시장이 보는 12월 인상 확률은 약 85%다. 증시는 견조해 S&P500이 6월 19일 7,501로 1% 상승했고 나스닥100은 약 2% 올랐다. 금은 약 4,150달러로 3주 연속 하락, 원유는 미국-이란 합의 기대로 약 9% 하락했다. 규제·제도 측면에서 한국 디지털자산기본법 2단계 입법은 6·3 지방선거 이후 하반기 재개가 기대된다. 원화 스테이블코인 발행 주체 요건과 거래소 대주주 지분 제한이 핵심 쟁점이며 연내 통과 여부는 불투명하다. 미국에서는 GENIUS Act 시행규정 마감 시한이 7월 18일로 다가왔고, 6개 기관의 의견수렴은 6월 9일 마감됐다. ETF 측면에서 미국 현물 비트코인 ETF는 30일 순유출 63억 5천만 달러로 사상 최대를 기록했으나, 주간 유출 속도는 6월 초 17억 달러대에서 직전 주 약 2억 달러대로 87% 둔화됐다. 주요 뉴스와 리스크로는 스트래티지(MSTR)가 2022년 12월 이후 처음으로 32 비트코인을 순매도한 뒤 세일러가 매집 재개를 시사한 점, 휴머니티 프로토콜 3,600만 달러 해킹과 지캐시 오차드 취약점 발견, 이더리움 샌드위치 봇 750만 달러 탈취 사건 등이 있다. 2026년 들어 디파이(DeFi) 해킹 누적 손실은 8억 4천만 달러를 넘어섰다. 상세 분석 시장 가격 상세 2026년 6월 셋째 주 암호화폐 시장은 변동성이 극도로 압축된 횡보장이었다. 비트코인은 6월 21일 약 6만 4천 달러에 마감했으며 일중 고가와 저가의 차이가 약 600달러에 불과했다. 이는 연초 6만에서 9만 8천 달러에 이르던 넓은 박스권에서 6월 중순 저변동 압축 국면으로 전환된 것을 보여준다. 이러한 변동성 압축은 통상 큰 방향성 움직임을 예고하는 신호로 해석되곤 한다. 비트코인은 6월 19일 한때 6만 2,200달러까지 하락했는데, 매파적 연준과 이란 협정 서명 지연이 겹친 결과였다. 주요 지지선은 6만 2천 달러 부근 200주 이동평균선이며 저항은 6만 5천에서 6만 6천 달러다. 200주 이동평균선은 역사적으로 강세장과 약세장의 주요 전환점으로 작동해온 만큼, 이 레벨의 방어 여부가 단기 방향성을 가른다. 이더리움은 약 1,720~1,730달러에서 거래되며 24시간 보합, 주간으로는 약 3% 상승했다. 모건스탠리가 0.14% 수수료의 이더리움 ETF를 신청했고 글램스테르담 업그레이드는 3분기로 목표가 이동했다. 이더리움/비트코인 비율은 약세 추세를 지속하며 하락 채널 저항에 막혀 있어, 자본이 이더리움보다 비트코인에 머물러 있음을 시사한다. 솔라나는 약 73~74달러로 24시간 2~4% 상승하며 연중 저점에서 반등 중이다. 시가총액 7위, 유통량 약 5억 8천만 개 수준이다. 알펜글로우(근사 즉시 완결성)와 파이어댄서 업그레이드가 3분기 핵심 촉매다. 모건스탠리는 1월 6일 SEC에 솔라나·비트코인·이더리움 신탁 양식을 제출하며 미국 대형 은행 최초로 자체 브랜드 현물 암호화폐 ETF를 신청했다. 다만 60만 솔라나가 거래소로 이동해 단기 매도 압력 우려도 제기됐다. 시장 전반 지표로는 비트코인 도미넌스가 약 58%, 알트코인 시즌 지수는 약 37(75 이상이 알트시즌 확인 기준)로 비트코인 시즌에 머물러 있다. 분석가들은 비트코인 도미넌스가 55% 아래로 지속 하락해야 광범위한 알트코인 순환매가 확인된다고 본다. 공포탐욕지수는 6월 8일 8에서 6월 15일 20으로 소폭 회복했으나 여전히 극단적 공포 영역이다. 다만 역사적으로 극단적 공포는 종종 중기 바닥과 일치해왔다. HYPE 및 하이퍼리퀴드 생태계 HYPE는 6월 16일 약 76.7달러로 사상 최고가를 경신했다. 6월 6일 토큰 언락 후 약 53달러까지 조정됐다가 다시 최고가를 기록한 흐름이다. 최고가 촉매로는 약 1,150만 달러 규모의 숏 스퀴즈, 플랫폼 수익의 90%로 HYPE를 매입하는 바이백 프로그램, 스페이스X 무기한 선물 거래량 급증, 현물 ETF 유입이 복합적으로 작용했다. 현재 가격은 69~70달러대로 최고가 대비 약 10% 낮으며 시가총액은 약 152억 달러로 10위권이다. 하이퍼리퀴드 생태계 모멘텀은 강하다. HIP-3 빌더 배포형 무기한 선물 미결제약정이 14억 달러를 돌파했고 토큰화 주식·원자재가 상위 30개 거래쌍 중 23개를 차지한다. 키네틱 런치(거래소형 서비스)가 6월 11일 메인넷에 출시됐으며, 키네틱 예치총액은 6월 초 11억 6천만 달러로 25일간 54% 증가하며 하이퍼리퀴드 유동성 스테이킹 시장의 85%를 점유한다. 현물 HYPE ETF는 5월 출시 후 순자산 2억 2천만 달러를 축적했고 6월 한 달 약 5천만 달러가 유입돼 같은 기간 리플 ETF를 상회했다. 이 수요는 연 10억 달러 이상의 거래 수수료를 HYPE 바이백에 투입하는 토크노믹스와 직결돼 있다. 다만 7월 6일 코어 기여자 언락 등 월간 공급 압력은 지속되는 리스크다. 거시·지정학 미국-이란 전쟁(미국 작전명 에픽 퓨리)은 2026년 2월 28일 개전했다. 4월 8일 파키스탄 중재로 휴전이 발효됐으나 산발적 교전이 이어졌고, 6월 15일 미국 측과 이란 측이 양해각서에 디지털 서명한 뒤 6월 19일 스위스 정식 서명이 예정됐다. 호르무즈 해협은 원칙적으로 재개통됐으나 6월 20~21일 이란의 재봉쇄 위협으로 불확실성이 다시 불거졌다. 해협 봉쇄 시 유가 급등에서 인플레이션 우려, 위험자산 압박으로 이어지는 경로가 작동할 수 있어 암호화폐의 핵심 거시 변수다. 비트코인은 평상시 불확실성 헤지로 묘사되지만, 급작스러운 지정학 충격 국면에서는 위험자산처럼 거래되는 경향이 있다. 연준은 6월 17일 금리를 3.50~3.75%로 동결했다. 워시 신임 의장의 첫 회의로, 점도표 연말 중앙값을 3.8%로 올리며 인상 시그널을 냈고 위원 18명 중 9명이 연내 인상을 전망했다. 5월 소비자물가지수는 4.2%로 3년 최고치였으며 전쟁발 에너지 가격 급등이 주요인이다. 달러 인덱스는 1년 최고치로 상승했고 시장은 12월 인상 확률을 약 85%로 본다. 강한 고용지표가 비트코인에 매도 신호로 작용하는 역설적 환경으로, 비트코인이 유동성과 금리 기대에 민감하게 반응함을 보여준다. 증시는 비교적 견조했다. S&P500은 6월 19일 7,501로 1% 상승, 나스닥100은 약 2% 올라 미국-이란 합의와 반도체 강세가 매파적 연준 우려를 상쇄했다. 금은 약 4,150달러로 3주 연속 하락했고(강달러·매파 연준 영향), 원유는 미국-이란 합의 기대로 약 9% 하락했다. 비트코인과 나스닥의 상관관계는 위험회피 국면에서 강화되는 양상이다. 규제 한국 디지털자산기본법 2단계 입법은 6·3 지방선거 종료 후 하반기 재개가 기대된다. 핵심 쟁점은 원화 스테이블코인 발행 주체 요건과 거래소 대주주 지분 제한이다. 여당 정무위 간사는 한 행사에서 연내 입법 추진 의지를 밝혔으나, 연내 통과는 정무위 원 구성 협상에 달려 있어 불투명하다. 정부 최종안은 2026년 초 제출이 전망된다. 미국에서는 GENIUS Act 시행규정 마감 시한이 법 제정일로부터 1년 후인 7월 18일이며, 6개 기관(통화감독청·연방예금보험공사·전미신용조합청·재무부·금융범죄단속네트워크·해외자산통제국)의 의견수렴은 6월 9일 마감됐다. 6월 연준이 고객신원확인 규칙을 제안했고, SEC와 CFTC는 3월 17일 비트코인·이더리움·솔라나·리플 등 16개 암호자산을 상품선물거래위원회 관할 디지털 상품으로 분류했다. ETF 측면에서 미국 현물 비트코인 ETF는 30일 순유출 63억 5천만 달러로 사상 최대를 기록했다. 6주 연속 유출이었으나 주간 유출 속도는 6월 초 17억 달러대에서 직전 주 약 2억 달러대로 87% 둔화됐다. 이는 가장 격렬한 기관 매도 국면이 지나가고 있을 가능성을 시사한다. 비트코인이 유출 둔화 속에 6만 4천 달러 부근을 지킨 점은 장기 보유자가 ETF 매물을 흡수했음을 의미한다. 리스크 6월 중 휴머니티 프로토콜 3,600만 달러 해킹(관리자 키 유출로 악성 컨트랙트 업그레이드 가능), 지캐시 오차드 인플레이션 취약점 발견(2022년부터 4년간 미발견, 실제 악용 증거는 없음), 이더리움 최대 샌드위치 봇 750만 달러 탈취 등이 발생했다. 휴머니티 프로토콜은 6월에 약 3,600만 달러 규모의 보안 사고를 겪었다. 2026년 들어 디파이 해킹 누적 손실은 8억 4천만 달러를 넘어섰으며 4월에만 6억 달러 이상이 발생했다. 4월 18일 켈프다오 2억 9,200만 달러, 4월 1일 드리프트 프로토콜 2억 8,500만 달러가 최대 사건이다. 조사기관에 따르면 북한 연계 행위자가 2026년 글로벌 해킹 손실의 약 76%를 차지하며, 공격 벡터가 스마트컨트랙트 버그에서 운영·키 탈취로 이동하고 있다. 기업 동향으로는 스트래티지(MSTR)가 5월 말 32 비트코인을 평균 약 7만 7천 달러에 매도했는데, 이는 2022년 12월 이후 첫 순매도로 공시됐다. 세일러는 이후 매집 재개를 시사했다. 매도 규모 자체는 전체 비트코인 보유고의 극히 일부로 미미하나, 대표적 비트코인 트레저리 기업의 매도라는 상징성이 컸다. 관전 포인트 단기 1~2주 흐름에서는 비트코인 6만 2천 달러(200주 이동평균선) 방어 여부가 핵심 관전 포인트다. 이 레벨을 이탈하면 추가 하락 위험이 있고, 6만 6천 달러를 실거래량과 함께 회복하면 심리가 반전될 여지가 있다. 매파적 연준과 ETF 유출이 상단을 제한하는 환경이다. 방향 전환의 주요 변수로는 호르무즈 재봉쇄가 현실화될 경우 유가와 인플레이션 급등으로 위험회피가 강화되는 시나리오와, 6월 19일 평화협정 정식 서명으로 위험선호가 회복되는 시나리오가 대비된다. 여기에 ETF 주간 순유입 전환 여부, 12월 연준 인상 기대의 완화 여부가 함께 작용한다. 알트코인 전반은 비트코인 도미넌스가 55%를 하향 이탈하고 알트코인 시즌 지수가 50을 넘기 전까지는 순환매보다 선별적 흐름이 우세할 가능성이 높다. HYPE는 수수료 바이백·ETF 유입·HIP-3 성장 등 펀더멘털이 강한 편이나 7월 6일 언락과 높은 완전희석가치가 부담 요인이며, 76.7달러 돌파 시 가격 발견 국면 진입이 가능하다. 워치리스트의 고FDV 토큰(비트웨이·벨벳·사하라 등)은 저유통·고FDV 구조상 언락 시 하방 압력이 크다는 점이 공통 특징이다. 사하라와 벨벳은 이미 대폭 조정됐으나 추가 언락 일정 확인이 필수다. 한국 투자자 관점에서는 김치 역프리미엄 지속 시 국내 매수세 약화 신호로 읽을 수 있으며, 디지털자산기본법 정무위 일정이 국내 제도화와 법인 투자 허용의 핵심 트리거이므로 하반기 입법 뉴스를 모니터링할 필요가 있다. 유의사항 가격 수치는 출처와 타임스탬프에 따라 편차가 있어(예: HYPE 최고가 76.6~76.8달러, 현재가 68~72달러), 본 리포트는 라이브 데이터와 1차 소스를 우선해 정리했다. 일부 가격예측성 페이지의 부정확한 오래된 데이터는 배제했다. 김치 프리미엄은 2026년 대부분 마이너스(역프리미엄)였으며, 빗썸의 부분 영업정지로 유동성이 업비트로 집중되면서 신호 신뢰도가 다소 저하된 상태다. 워치리스트 토큰 중 'JCT'는 하이퍼리퀴드 생태계가 아닌 BNB체인 인공지능 레이어2 '잰션'으로 확인됐으며, 'BTW'는 하이퍼리퀴드 HIP-3가 아닌 비트코인 기반 토큰 '비트웨이'다. 지정학·규제·ETF 흐름은 유동적이며 본 리포트는 6월 21일 시점 정보로, 미국-이란 정식 서명과 연준 정책 경로, 호르무즈 상황은 이후 변경될 수 있다. 본 리포트는 특정 종목의 매수나 매도를 권유하는 것이 아니라 순수하게 정보 제공을 목적으로 작성되었다. 암호화폐는 변동성이 매우 큰 자산이므로 모든 투자 판단과 그에 따른 책임은 투자자 본인에게 있으며, 투자 전 반드시 독립적인 검증을 거치기 바란다.

Defi를 믿을 수 없다면, 이제는 @CapApp  이 나서야 할 때 ( @Benjamin918_  and @fireant_korea 인터뷰 영상 후기)  제 크립토 경력은 길지 않습니다. 이제 막 2년차를 맞이하고 있으나, DeFi가 지금 얼마나 위험한 상황에 처했는지는 알고 있습니다.  개인의 실수로 인해 해킹당하는 것 이외에도, 프로토콜이 해킹을 당한다거나, 혹은 러그풀로 인해 돈이 사라지는 등 그야말로 탈중앙화를 빌미로 한 무법천지의 세상이 되어버렸죠.  더욱더 비극인 점은 CAP의 파운더인 Benjamin의 말처럼 프로토콜 스스로의 도덕적 해이입니다.   Fantom의 대출 프로토콜 중 한 프로젝트(Scream)에서 핵심 관계자가 러그풀을 하고 싶다는 등 입에 담기 힘든 말을 하기도 했으며, 실제로 Scream이라는 프로토콜은 fUSD와 DEI라는 스테이블코인의 디페깅을 악용한 차익거래로 풀이 고갈되어 말 그대로 무너져버리고 말았습니다.  오라클과 한도 설정을 잘못한 리스크 관리의 실패라는, DeFi의 뻔하디뻔한 결말 중 하나지만 여기에는 프로토콜과 그 관계자들조차 믿어선 안 된다는 교훈을 담고 있습니다.  현재 CAP의 CTO인 Weso도 당시의 위험을 옆에서 겪었고, 간신히 자금을 빼내는 데에 성공해 피해를 줄였다고 하죠. 그리고 그 교훈이 Cap으로 이어지게 되었습니다.  Cap에서 발행하는 cUSD라는 스테이블코인은, 디페깅을 구조에서 막아두었습니다. 0.05%라는 타이트한 가격 오차 범위를 설정해둔 것은 물론이고, 상환 구조에서도 방지해두었습니다. cUSD를 되찾을 때 담보 바스켓 전체를 비율대로 그대로 돌려주어, 늦게 빠져나온 사람이 디페깅된 자산을 다 떠안는 문제도 방지했죠.  여기에 그치지 않고 Benjamin이 말하듯 CAP은 '편집증적'이라 할 수 있는 보안 시스템을 추가했습니다. Beefy 출신 엔지니어들이 대거 합류했기에, 시스템 붕괴의 두려움을 누구보다 잘 알고 있기 때문일 겁니다.  단순히 핵심 관계자들이 보안에 신경 써야 한다는 수준의 뻔하디 뻔한 격언 같은 게 아니라, AI 코드 감사 도구인 Octane으로 배포 전 코드를 일일이 검증하고, 실시간 모니터링 시스템인 Hypernative를 도입해 멤풀을 감시합니다.  해킹 징후가 잡히는 즉시, 사람 손을 거치지 않아도 프로토콜 전체가 즉시 멈추게 되어있는데, 그것도 24시간 365일 내내 감시가 됩니다. 여기에 Certora 정식 감사와 멀티시그, 추가 서명 절차 같은 기본도 당연히 갖췄습니다.  CAP의 특징은 수익원도 크립토에 한정하지 않겠다는 겁니다. 현재 자본 시장의 자금은 AI와 반도체로 흐르고 있습니다. 크립토 안에서 한정된 자원을 두고 수익을 만드는 건 한계에 봉착했죠.   그래서 CAP은 실물 경제로 눈을 돌려 로보틱스와 농업, 제조 분야의 실물 대출 시장과 함께 중동의 RWA 시장으로까지 확대하려는 로드맵을 그리고 있습니다.   토큰화된 금과 디르함 기반 대출 상품이 준비된다면 DeFi가 온체인에서 머무르지 않고 오프체인까지 아우르는 금융 자산이 될 수도 있을 듯합니다.  DeFi를 복잡하고 위험해서 멀리했던 사람들에게 어떻게 접근해야 하는지, CAP은 한국 시장의 이용자들에 대한 관심을 통해 방법을 제시하는 것 같습니다. 스테이블코인조차도 리스크는 0이 아니지만, Cap은 이를 좀 더 '접근 가능한 금융상품'으로 제공하기 위해 노력하는 것처럼 보입니다.

BYD OTA 업데이트 공지. (6월 19일 공지)  ATTO 3, 씰, 돌핀 전 라인업에 6월 19일 자로 대규모 패치가 떴는데요.  스마트폰이 아니라 시속 100km로 달리는 '움직이는 기기'가 무선으로 업데이트라......  이번 업데이트, 단순 UI 개선이 아닙니다. 지능형 크루즈 컨트롤(ICC),  코너 주행 시 감속 로직까지— 주행 제어 영역을 손댔습니다.  테슬라가 연 '소프트웨어 중심 자동차(SDV)' 시대, 제조사에겐 비용 절감이지만 소비자에겐 보안 리스크의 시작입니다.  중국산 전기차 백도어 우려, 단순 반중 정서일까요?  작년 글로벌 자동차 보안 기업들의 화이트 해킹 실험 결과는 충격적이었습니다. 차량 제어 내부망(CAN 버스)에 침투하는 순간 브레이크를 풀고 조향 장치를 꺾는 것까지  원격으로 가능했습니다.  BYD도 'UN R155' 같은 국제 사이버보안 규제를 맞추며 한국 진출을 준비 중이지만, 무선 연결(OTA) 통로가 열려있다는 것 자체가 해커에겐 가장 매력적인 타겟입니다.  편의성을 얻은 대가로 우리는 '달리는 해킹 위험'을  떠안게 된 셈입니다.  기능이 화려해질수록 차를 살 때 '보안 등급'부터 따져야 하는 날이 머지않았습니다.  무선 업데이트되는 차, 100% 신뢰하고 타실 수 있나요?  📌 나중에 차 고를 때 참고하도록  북마크 해두세요.

미국이 직접 조치를 시작할때는 단순한 한미공조 수사가 아니라 미국을 주축으로 부정선거에 피해를 입은 국가들이 연합한 국제 합동 부정선거 수사단을 출범시켜 국제적으로 압박해 들어올 가능성이 높습니다.   아래는 가상 시나리오로 AI에 물어본 내용입니다. ㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡㅡ  <출처. 구글 AI> 네, 그러한 가상의 시나리오에서도 미국을 비롯한 피해국들이 합동 수사단을 구성해 대한민국 정부나 개입 기관, 관련자들을 수사할 가능성은 매우 높습니다.  실제 국제사회에서 러시아, 중국, 이란 등이 타국 선거에 개입했을 때 적용되었던 선례와 국제법적 절차를 바탕으로 볼 때, 가상의 상황에서 발생할 수 있는 구체적인 수사 가능성과 시나리오는 다음과 같습니다.  1. 다국적 합동 수사단(JIT) 구성 가능성 여러 국가가 동시에 동일한 주체(가상의 시나리오 속 한국)로부터 선거 개입 피해를 입었다면, 각국은 정보와 사법 역량을 결합합니다.  ◈ 증거 공유 미국, 영국, 캐나다 등 정보 동맹국들이 사이버 공격 기법(IP 주소, 악성코드 시그니처)과 자금 흐름을 공동 추적합니다.  ◈ 공동 수사 체계 유로폴(Europol)이나 파이브 아이즈(Five Eyes) 시스템을 기반으로 한 민관 합동 수사단이 조직되어 한국 내 특정 기관(예: 정보기관이나 군 사이버 부대)을 표적으로 수사를 전개합니다.  2. 한국 사법당국에 대한 강제적·협조적 수사 요구 피해국 합동 수사단이 한국 영토 내에서 직접 체포나 압수수색을 할 수는 없지만, 강력한 법적 조치를 취합니다.  ◈ 사법공조(MLAT) 압박 미국 법무부 등은 한국 검찰과 법원에 선거 개입에 사용된 서버 압수수색, 관련자 신문 등 공식 사법공조를 요구합니다. 가상의 상황에서 한국 정부가 거부할 경우 외교적 고립과 보복 조치가 따르므로 무시하기 어렵습니다.  ◈ 범죄인 인도 청구 개입을 주도한 해커나 공작원의 신원이 특정되면, 피해국들은 자국법(예: 미국의 컴퓨터 사기 및 남용법 위반, 선거 자금법 위반 등) 체계에 따라 한국 정부에 범죄인 인도를 청구합니다.  3. 피의자 신원 공개 및 독자 기소 (궐석기소) 한국 정부가 주권이나 국가 안보를 이유로 합동 수사단의 수사에 협조하지 않더라도, 피해국들은 독자적인 사법 절차를 강행합니다.  ◈ 미 연방검찰(DOJ)의 기소 미국 FBI와 법무부는 선거 개입 배후 인물들의 실명과 사진을 공소장에 명시하고 현지 법원에 기소합니다. 인신 확보가 안 되더라도 체포영장을 발부받아 국제적으로 고립시킵니다.  ◈ 인터폴 적색수배 합동 수사단의 요청으로 연루자들에게 인터폴 적색수배가 내려지며, 이들이 한국 영토 밖으로 출국하는 순간 제3국에서 체포되어 미국 등으로 인도됩니다.  4. 사법 수사를 넘어선 고강도 제재 조치 합동 수사단의 수사 결과 발표는 대규모 국제 제재의 명분이 됩니다.  ◈ 금융 및 자산 동결 미국 대통령의 선거 개입 제재 행정명령 등에 따라, 개입에 관여한 한국의 기관, 공직자, 기업의 해외 자산이 동결되고 국제 금융망(SWIFT) 이용이 제한될 수 있습니다.  혹시 이 가상의 시나리오에서 선거 개입의 방식(예: 사이버 해킹, 가짜뉴스 유포, 자금 지원)이나 특정 국가들의 동맹 관계를 염두에 두신 것이 있나요? 구체적인 상황을 알려주시면 이에 맞춰 더 정확한 국제법적 대응 결과를 분석해 드리겠습니다.

Okay, the silly shenanigans are over. I've repaired the damage done to my box. Overall this was a very whimsical Saturday.  For people new to this account: Do I really not use a VM when doing malware analysis? Yes. I have a VM, but I don't know, I get lazy. I throw the malware in a random directory called like, "sgsdggggg" (I type random letters on the keyboard) and start poking it.  Is this the first time I've detonated malware on my computer? No. I've detonated ransomware (REvil), some suspected state-sponsored malware from the Russian government (APT28), a crypto-miner, and now this.  I also once messed up an rsync command and synced my tax documents to the staff at DEFCON. They expected malware, instead they got my birth certificate, my tax returns, and some other various documents.  Do I panic? No, I deal with malware every single day. I collect malware, reverse engineer malware, write malware, etc. It is more annoying than actually concerning. The only time I was genuinely concerned was when I detonated REvil because I usually don't make backups (I'm not a coward).  Basically, I'm a jackass who has gotten way too comfortable with malware and you should not do what I do.

Strong AI Requires Strong Infrastructure.   Organizations often focus on AI capabilities while overlooking the security and resilience of the infrastructure supporting them.   On this episode of DailyCyber, Ben Wilcox shares lessons learned from helping enterprises secure complex cloud and hybrid environments while embracing emerging technologies.   Key discussion points:  • Infrastructure resilience in modern enterprises  • Cloud security considerations for AI workloads  • Emerging attack surfaces created by automation  • Security architecture best practices  • Aligning technology growth with cyber resilience   Technology innovation moves quickly. Infrastructure failures move even faster when resilience isn't built into the design.   Guest: Ben Wilcox — CTO & CISO, ProArch   Host: Brandon Krieger — CEO & vCISO Advisor   📅 Tuesday at 5:00 PM EST  📺 LinkedIn.com/in/BrandonKrieg… #DailyCyber294, #CloudSecurity, #InfrastructureSecurity, #CyberResilience, #EnterpriseArchitecture, #CyberStrategy

🚨 CYBER INTELLIGENCE ALERT: ALLEGED DATA KIDNAPPING AND MASSIVE EXFILTRATION IN THE HEALTHTECH SECTOR — COLOMBIA 🇨🇴  [STATUS: ACTIVE THREAT / EXTORTION UNDERWAY / UNCONFIRMED / SOURCE: UNDERGROUND FORUM]  The threat actor identified by the alias Kazu has announced a massive intrusion and theft of the central repositories of SaludTools, a leading healthcare technology (HealthTech) firm in Colombia specializing in electronic health record management and medical center administration. The attacker claims to have exfiltrated a critical volume of 2.3 TB of structured and confidential information, fragmented into 4,599,294 separate files.  🏢 Allegedly Affected Entity: SaludTools / Healthcare Management System (Colombia — saludtools.com).  👤 Threat Actor: Kazu.  ⚔️ Potential Attack Vector: Infiltration of cloud storage servers (AWS/Azure) through API key leaks, bypassing access policies in production databases, or prior hijacking of development administrative credentials.  🔍 Verification Status: UNCONFIRMED. As of June 22, 2026, neither the technology company nor Colombian government cybersecurity agencies have issued any breach reports or containment plans. However, the alert is being processed at the perimeter severity level due to the actor's operational history and the inherent massive impact of a breach in an ecosystem that centralizes electronic health records (EHR/EMR).  🛡️ TECHNICAL RECOMMENDATIONS AND EMERGENCY RESPONSE  🛑 Forensic Audit of Storage Repositories (SOC Action): SaludTools infrastructure and security engineers are urged to urgently inspect access and data transfer logs (egress logs) in their storage buckets (e.g., Amazon S3) and database servers, looking for anomalous concurrent connections that could account for the mass download of terabytes of data.  🔑 Global Token Revocation and API Key Rotation: Immediately invalidate all active access sessions, session tokens, and cryptographic secrets. Enforce a mandatory password reset and Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) for all physician, administrator, and developer accounts with platform access.  📊 MONITORING AND ASSESSMENT  Intelligence System: analyzer.vecert.io  Quickly assess your website's security at: monitor.vecert.io #CyberSecurity #Colombia #SaludTools #HealthTech #Ransomware #MedicalRecords #DataLeak #PIIExposure #KazuThreat #EMRBreach #ThreatIntelligence #CyberAlert #VECERT #Infosec #UnverifiedBreach

The most important lesson from this week's cybersecurity headlines isn't what happened—it's what organizations do next.  Cybersecurity maturity isn't measured by avoiding every attack. It's measured by how effectively you respond when one occurs.  The organizations that perform best have made resilience part of their culture.  As AI accelerates both attacks and defenses, prepared people may become a greater advantage than any tool.  What are leaders still underestimating about cyber resilience?

Minnesota bans crypto ATMs after scam surge | Kurt Knutsson, Fox News  Minnesota is banning crypto ATMs after scammers reportedly used the machines to drain nearly $1 million from residents over the past few years. The machines, also called cryptocurrency kiosks, let people turn cash into digital currency quickly. That speed has made them a favorite tool for criminals who pressure victims during fake emergencies, legal threats and romance scams.  The ban takes effect Aug. 1, 2026. Operators must remove publicly accessible machines by the end of the year. State officials say scammers have used these kiosks to turn panic into payments. A victim gets a frightening call, heads to a machine and sends cash before anyone has time to step in. That quick window is exactly what criminals count on and what Minnesota wants to stop.  Minnesota crypto ATM ban targets fast-moving scams  Between 2023 and 2025, Minnesota logged 134 complaints involving crypto kiosk scams. Reported losses came close to $1 million. In 2025 alone, the state reportedly saw 70 cases and more than $540,000 in losses. Those numbers likely tell only part of the story. Many victims never report what happened. Some feel embarrassed. Others worry their family will judge them. Scammers know that silence helps them move on to the next target.  Minnesota had already tried adding safeguards to these machines. The state required warnings, limits and consumer protections. Yet law enforcement officials said scammers adjusted. They stayed on the phone with victims, coached them through the screens and told them what to say if anyone asked questions. Now, Minnesota is taking a much stronger step by removing the machines from public use.  Why crypto ATM scams are so hard to undo  Crypto kiosks create a dangerous mix for scam victims. They accept cash, move money quickly and usually leave very little room for recovery once the transaction goes through. With a bank transfer or credit card payment, there may be some chance to pause, dispute or trace the money. Crypto works differently. Once the digital currency leaves the wallet, the transfer can move across borders or through multiple wallets before anyone knows what happened.  That leaves victims in a brutal position. They may realize the call was fake only minutes after sending the money. By then, the cash has already been converted to crypto, and the scammer has likely moved it again. That is why scammers love these machines. They do not need to hack your bank account. They only need to scare you enough to follow their instructions.  How crypto ATM scams trap victims  Most crypto ATM scams begin with pressure. The caller may claim you missed jury duty and face arrest. Another scammer may pretend your grandchild caused an accident and needs bail money. Someone posing as a bank employee may say your account is under attack and your money needs to be "protected." The story changes, but the pressure feels the same. The scammer wants you scared, rushed and alone.  Then comes the payment demand. You may be told to withdraw cash and drive to a nearby crypto kiosk. The scammer may text you a QR code or wallet address. They may stay on the phone the entire time and tell you to ignore warnings on the screen. That last part is key. Many victims do see alerts. They still move forward because the scammer has created fear and urgency. In that moment, the warning on the machine has to compete with someone shouting that a loved one is in trouble or that police are on the way.  Real crypto scam cases show how fast money can disappear  We have seen this pattern before. The scam starts with a believable story, then the pressure builds until the victim feels there is no time to think.  Gail Barr lost $9,260 after scammers convinced her she had missed jury duty. They sent her to a Bitcoin ATM inside a convenience store and kept her on the phone while she followed their instructions. Thankfully, a bank manager later helped stop the scam from getting worse.  Then there was the 85-year-old man who lost $200,000 after a fake PayPal refund scheme pulled him into a much bigger trap. What began as a $10,000 crypto ATM transfer eventually turned into gold coins handed to a courier.  And in another scam, Joe Allen, a disabled man from Connecticut, shared how he lost more than $300,000 in a cryptocurrency investment scam. His story shows how criminals keep working someone once they sense fear, trust or isolation.  That is what makes these scams so dangerous. Most victims do not start out thinking they are sending money to a criminal. They believe they are solving a crisis, protecting their account or helping someone they care about. By the time the truth sinks in, the money may already be gone.  Crypto ATM scam losses are rising nationwide  Minnesota is hardly alone. The FBI says its Internet Crime Complaint Center received more than 13,400 complaints in 2025 involving cryptocurrency kiosks. Reported losses topped $388 million. More than half of those complaints involved people over 50, with losses above $302 million.  That age pattern should make every family pay attention. Older adults may have savings. They may answer calls from unknown numbers more often. They may also have a deep instinct to help a child or grandchild in trouble. Scammers weaponize that instinct.  The machine is only one part of the scam. The real attack happens in the victim's head. Once fear takes over, even careful people can make a decision they would never make on a calm day.  Minnesota's crypto ATM ban could influence other states  Minnesota is drawing a line in the sand around one part of the crypto world that has become a real problem for scam victims: the public kiosk. People in the state will still be able to use regulated online platforms to buy and sell cryptocurrency. What is going away are the machines that let a frightened person walk into a store, feed in cash and send crypto while a scammer talks them through every tap.  Other states will likely be watching what happens next. If Minnesota's ban cuts down on reported losses, lawmakers elsewhere may face more pressure to take a closer look at crypto kiosks in gas stations, convenience stores and shopping centers.  Supporters of the ban say that matters because these kiosks have become too easy for criminals to abuse. Critics say the machines have legitimate uses, and scammers will simply move to another payment method. That may be true. Scammers always look for the next opening. But Minnesota is betting that removing one of the fastest cash-to-crypto routes will slow the scam down enough for some victims to stop, question the story and keep their money.  Tips to avoid crypto ATM scams  The best way to protect yourself is to slow the moment down before fear turns into a payment you cannot easily reverse.  1) Hang up when someone demands crypto  No real police department, court, government agency or bank will demand payment through a crypto ATM. A legitimate company will not tell you to protect your account by feeding cash into a kiosk. If someone demands crypto, end the call. Do not debate. Do not explain. Hang up and contact the person or agency through a number you find yourself.  2) Verify the emergency before sending money  Scammers often claim a loved one is hurt, arrested or stranded. That story is designed to short-circuit your judgment. Call the person directly. If they do not answer, call another family member. A few minutes of checking can save thousands of dollars. A family code word can also help. Pick a phrase that would never appear on social media. If someone claims to be a loved one in trouble, ask for the code word before you do anything.  3) Reduce the personal info scammers can use against you  Scammers often sound convincing because they already know something about you or your family. That information can come from people-search sites, data brokers, old breaches or public records. Consider using a data removal service to reduce how much personal information is floating around online.   4) Watch for coaching at the machine  If someone tells you what to tap, what to ignore or what to say if a clerk asks questions, you are likely in the middle of a scam. That coaching is a major red flag. Scammers know store employees and bank workers may spot the danger. So they prepare victims with cover stories before anyone can intervene.  5) Use strong antivirus software to block scam links  Some crypto scams start with a fake support page, phishing link or malicious download. Strong antivirus software can help block dangerous sites and files before they pull you into a scam.   6) Talk to your bank before withdrawing cash  Large cash withdrawals should trigger a pause. Tell your bank what happened before you leave with the money. Bank employees see these scams often. In some cases, one question from a teller or manager can break through the fear and stop the loss.  7) Consider identity theft protection after a scam  If you have already sent money or shared sensitive information, identity theft protection can help monitor for new account fraud and alert you to suspicious activity.   8) Report the scam quickly  If you sent money through a crypto kiosk, report it right away. Contact local police, your state consumer protection office and the FBI's Internet Crime Complaint Center at IC3.gov. The FBI says IC3 is its main intake form for cyber-enabled fraud and scams, even if you are unsure whether your complaint qualifies. Fast reporting may help investigators spot patterns and warn others before the same scam hits another family.  Read more:  foxnews.com/tech/minnesota-b…
