Why America Can Devastate Iran but Still Cannot Defeat It  The United States can dominate Iranian airspace, destroy military installations, damage nuclear facilities, sink naval assets and eliminate commanders. Yet that does not amount to comprehensive victory.  The problem is political, not military. Washington would have to eliminate Iran’s nuclear capability, missile programme, regional networks and ruling structure, then create a successor government. Air power can destroy buildings, but not knowledge, identity or the will to resist.  Iran is not Iraq or Libya. It is a vast, mountainous country of more than 90 million people with entrenched institutions. Occupying it would require huge ground forces, heavy casualties and prolonged state-building with no guarantee of success.  Iran’s strategy is asymmetric. It does not need to defeat the U.S. military. It only needs to make war costly and prolonged through missiles, drones, cyber operations, regional allies and threats to shipping through the Strait of Hormuz.  Time also works differently. For Tehran, survival is existential. For Washington, Iran is one challenge among many. A weaker state can outlast a stronger one by raising the political and military cost of victory.  Regime change is equally uncertain. Iran’s system extends beyond one leader, while the opposition is fragmented and may not be ready to govern. External attack can strengthen nationalism and help the regime portray dissent as foreign collaboration.  America can coerce Iran, but coercion is not control. It can devastate what Iran has today, yet cannot guarantee what follows tomorrow.  The deeper lesson is simple: military superiority is not political mastery. America can punish, delay and weaken Iran, but unless it is prepared to occupy and administer the country indefinitely, it cannot determine Iran’s future.  By Sanjay K Verma – Seasoned Textile Technologist, Political Analyst, and Conscious Community Voice

🔍 Day 10 of the Fable 5 shutdown, and the real reason has finally come into focus. It is messier than the early "jailbreak" story suggested.  Two separate events converged to trigger the June 12 export controls: 1️⃣ The White House learned that SK Telecom, South Korea's largest carrier (and an Anthropic investor), had gained access to Mythos 5 through Project Glasswing, and suspected undisclosed ties to China. SK Telecom denies this, and no evidence has been made public. #Anthropic revoked its access immediately. 2️⃣ Separately, Amazon (Anthropic's largest investor at ~$13B) flagged a guardrail bypass in Fable 5 to the White House after researchers turned it into a vulnerability-finding tool. CEO Andy Jassy reportedly raised it with officials directly.  Where it stands today: - Both models are still offline globally. Claude Opus 4.8, Sonnet 4.6, and Haiku 4.5 were never affected - Anthropic says it is "very confident" access returns "in the coming days." Prediction markets price roughly 57% odds before July 1 - But new Senate testimony tied to the NSA is reportedly extending the standoff - About 100 security experts, including Alex Stamos and Katie Moussouris, signed an open letter arguing Mythos-class models are "quite good" but "not uniquely good" at finding flaws, urging the controls be lifted  And a quiet milestone today: June 22 is when the subscription-included window was set to close. Whenever Fable 5 returns, continued use shifts to usage credits at API rates.  The takeaway holds: a frontier model is now a geopolitical asset, and access can change overnight. 🚀  -------  🔍 Fable 5 kapanışında 10. gün ve asıl sebep nihayet netleşti. İlk "jailbreak" hikayesinin ima ettiğinden daha karmaşık.  12 Haziran ihracat kontrollerini tetikleyen iki ayrı olay birleşti: 1️⃣ White House, Güney Kore'nin en büyük operatörü (ve bir Anthropic yatırımcısı) SK Telecom'un Project Glasswing üzerinden Mythos 5 erişimi aldığını öğrendi ve Çin ile açıklanmamış bağlantılardan şüphelendi. SK Telecom bunu reddediyor, kamuya açıklanmış bir kanıt yok. #Anthropic erişimi hemen iptal etti. 2️⃣ Ayrıca Amazon (Anthropic'in ~13 milyar dolarla en büyük yatırımcısı), araştırmacılar Fable 5'i bir açık bulma aracına dönüştürdükten sonra modeldeki bir guardrail bypass'ını White House'a bildirdi. CEO Andy Jassy'nin konuyu doğrudan yetkililere taşıdığı belirtiliyor.  Bugün durum şöyle: - İki model de hâlâ dünya genelinde offline. Claude Opus 4.8, Sonnet 4.6 ve Haiku 4.5 hiç etkilenmedi - Anthropic erişimin "önümüzdeki günlerde" döneceğinden "çok emin" olduğunu söylüyor. Tahmin piyasaları 1 Temmuz öncesi için yaklaşık %57 olasılık fiyatlıyor - Ama NSA'ya bağlı yeni bir Senato ifadesi gerginliği uzatıyor - Aralarında Alex Stamos ve Katie Moussouris'in de olduğu yaklaşık 100 güvenlik uzmanı açık mektup imzaladı: Mythos sınıfı modeller açık bulmada "oldukça iyi" ama "benzersiz derecede iyi değil", kontroller kaldırılsın  22 Haziran, aboneliğe dahil pencerenin kapanacağı gündü. Fable 5 ne zaman dönerse dönsün, sonraki kullanım API fiyatlı usage credit sistemine geçiyor.  Sonuç değişmiyor: bir frontier model artık jeopolitik bir varlık ve erişim bir gecede değişebilir. 🚀  #AI #Anthropic #ClaudeFable5 #AIpolicy #ExportControls

AI가 알트코인을 때리는 방식은 밸류에이션 압축이 아니라 신뢰 붕괴다  앞글에서 소프트웨어 주식은 AI 에이전트 때문에 리레이팅 압력을 받을 수 있다고 썼다.  그런데 알트코인은 다르다. 알트코인은 멀티플 압축의 문제가 아니다.  신뢰 붕괴의 문제다.  최근 Zcash 사례가 그걸 보여줬다.  Zcash는 오래된 프라이버시 코인이다. 그런데 Orchard shielded pool에서 치명적 취약점이 발견됐다. 해당 취약점은 이론적으로 비탐지 위조 ZEC 생성을 가능하게 할 수 있는 문제였다. 실제 악용 증거는 없었고 긴급 패치도 됐다. 그런데도 시장은 바로 던졌다.  왜냐하면 코인은 신뢰가 가격이기 때문이다.  주식은 실적이 꺾여도 남는 것이 있다.  고객. 현금흐름. 공장. 특허. 브랜드. 사업부 매각 가치.  하지만 많은 알트코인은 신뢰가 깨지면 남는 것이 거의 없다.  특히 DeFi, 브릿지, 오라클, 레버리지 프로토콜, AI코인, DePIN, 분산 GPU 토큰 같은 영역은 더 위험하다.  이들의 가치는 대부분 기대와 신뢰 위에 서 있다.  “이 프로토콜은 안전하다.” “TVL이 유지된다.” “유동성이 빠지지 않는다.” “토큰이 생태계 가치를 가져간다.” “해킹당하지 않는다.”  이 전제가 깨지는 순간 가격은 천천히 리레이팅되는 게 아니라 한 번에 무너질 수 있다.  AI가 여기서 중요한 이유는 공격자의 비용을 낮추기 때문이다.  예전에는 스마트컨트랙트 취약점을 찾으려면 고급 보안 인력, 시간, 경험, 수작업 분석이 필요했다.  앞으로는 다르다.  AI 에이전트가 코드를 읽는다. 취약점을 찾는다. 공격 경로를 시뮬레이션한다. 로컬 환경에서 exploit을 테스트한다. 자동으로 반복한다.  물론 AI가 모든 프로토콜을 자동으로 털 수 있다는 뜻은 아니다. 하지만 투자 관점에서는 그 정도까지 필요 없다.  방어자는 모든 구멍을 막아야 한다. 공격자는 하나만 찾으면 된다.  이게 비대칭이다.  소형 알트는 특히 취약하다.  작은 개발팀. 부족한 보안 예산. 복잡한 토크노믹스. 브릿지와 오라클 의존. 짧은 감사 기간. 얇은 유동성. 실사용보다 큰 내러티브.  이 상태에서 한 번 사고가 나면 끝이다.  프로토콜 금고가 털린다. TVL이 빠진다. LP가 도망간다. 거래소 유동성이 마른다. 토큰 가격이 급락한다. 팀 신뢰가 무너진다. 커뮤니티가 붕괴한다.  그다음은 사실상 0에 가까워질 수 있다.  지금 코인판은 이미 돈이 강하게 들어오는 장이 아니다.  비트코인도 2026년 들어 약세다. 디지털자산 펀드에서도 자금 유출이 나오고 있다. 투자자들은 AI 주식, 반도체, 데이터센터, 대형 IPO 쪽으로 눈을 돌리고 있다.  이런 상황에서 AI가 코인의 취약점을 찾아낸다는 뉴스는 단순 악재가 아니다.  관짝에 못 박는 뉴스에 가깝다.  왜냐하면 알트코인 시장의 핵심 내러티브가 흔들리기 때문이다.  “탈중앙이라 안전하다.” “코드가 곧 법이다.” “감사받았으니 괜찮다.” “커뮤니티가 크니까 버틴다.”  그런데 AI가 사람이 몇 년 동안 못 찾은 취약점을 찾아내기 시작하면 이 전제들이 약해진다.  AI 발전은 코인판에 양면성이 있다. 방어도 좋아진다. 감사도 빨라진다. 취약점 탐지도 쉬워진다. 하지만 공격도 좋아진다. 해커도 AI를 쓴다. 소형 프로젝트의 방어 격차는 더 커진다.  결국 강한 프로젝트와 약한 프로젝트의 차이는 더 벌어진다.  BTC는 별개로 봐야 한다. BTC는 스마트컨트랙트 해킹 리스크보다 유동성, 금리, 디지털 금 내러티브로 움직인다.  ETH, SOL 같은 대형 체인도 소형 알트와 다르다. 체인 자체가 바로 0이 된다기보다, 위에 올라간 DeFi와 앱들이 계속 공격받는 구조다.  가장 위험한 것은 소형 알트, DeFi governance token, 브릿지 토큰, AI 내러티브 코인, 실사용 없는 DePIN 토큰이다.  AI라는 단어가 붙었다고 수혜가 아니다.  진짜 AI 병목은 GPU, HBM, 전력, 데이터센터, 모델 접근권, 보안이다.  토큰을 발행했다고 프론티어 모델을 만들 수 있는 게 아니다. 분산 GPU 내러티브가 있다고 NVIDIA, HBM, 데이터센터 병목을 이길 수 있는 게 아니다. 데이터 토큰이 있다고 고품질 데이터와 기업 고객이 생기는 게 아니다.  AI가 발전할수록 오히려 프론티어 모델과 컴퓨트는 대형 기업, 클라우드, 정부 파트너로 더 집중될 가능성이 높다.  그럼 많은 AI 알트코인은 수혜주가 아니라 내러티브 자산으로 재평가될 수 있다.  결론은 단순하다. AI가 소프트웨어를 때리는 방식은 멀티플 압축이다. AI가 알트코인을 때리는 방식은 신뢰 붕괴다.  소프트웨어는 좋은 기업이 싸지면 나중에 살아날 수 있다. 하지만 알트코인은 한 번 신뢰가 깨지면 회복 자체가 불가능한 경우가 많다.  AI 시대에 강해지는 것은 “AI라는 이름을 붙인 자산”이 아니다.  AI를 실제로 생산하고, 운반하고, 보호하는 인프라다.  GPU. HBM. 전력. 냉각. 데이터센터. 네트워크. 사이버보안. 권한관리.  반대로 약해지는 것은 신뢰만으로 버티는 자산이다. 소프트웨어에서는 얇은 앱이 약하고, 크립토에서는 실체 없는 알트코인이 약하다.  향후 이 흐름은 더 가속화될 가능성이 높다.  AI 모델이 강해질수록 취약점 탐색 비용은 낮아지고, 보안 체력이 약한 프로젝트는 계속 압박받을 수밖에 없다.  시장은 결국 이 차이를 가격에 반영할 것이다.

📢 [공통세션 조별 리서치 발표 - 코어팀 / 4월 9일]  오늘 발표에서는 QED Audit이 보고한 CometBFT의 Time-Warp 취약점을 분석하고, 직접 재현했습니다.  합의 로직을 코드 레벨에서 추적하며, 이 취약점이 PoS 체인 전반에 어떤 영향을 미칠 수 있는지 깊이 있게 파고든 발표였습니다.  📌 핵심 내용 1. CometBFT 합의 흐름 전반의 함수 호출 및 상호작용 분석 2. 취약점 원인: Commit 검증(인덱스 기반) vs MedianTime() 계산(주소 기반)의 조회 방식 불일치 3. 경제적 영향: 블록 간 시간 차이 비례 보상 구조를 악용한 단일 공격으로 대규모 보상 탈취 가능 4. 공격 시나리오 재현: 악의적 Validator가 단 한 번의 Block Propose 기회만으로 공격 성공 확인  💬 핵심 메시지 취약점을 "발견"하는 것과 "이해"하는 것은 다르다는 점을 확인했습니다. 이번 발표는 코드 레벨 추적을 통해 그 차이를 파악하는 데 중점을 두었습니다.  📍 장소 지원: 퓨처하우스 @FutureHouseKR #BlockchainValley #블록체인밸리 #CometBFT #TimeWarp #블록체인보안 #Cosmos #Web3

“선관위 해킹 취약...개표 결과도 조작 가능”  국정원은 외부에서 선관위 내부망에 침투해 투표 여부 또는 선거인명부를 조작하거나, 개표 결과값까지 변경할 수 있는 것으로 나타났습니다.   - 2023년 10월 10일 KBS 뉴스 보도 내용  국정원이 한국인터넷진흥원과 합동점검을 한 결과, 선거인명부시스템과 개표시스템, 사전투표시스템 전반에 걸쳐 해킹 위험에 노출된 것으로 나타났습니다.  먼저 선거인명부시스템의 경우, 외부에서 내부망에 침투해 유령 유권자를 등록하는 등 명부를 바꾸거나 투표 여부도 임의로 바꿀 수 있었습니다.  또 내부 시스템에 침투한 뒤 선관위의 날인 파일을 도용해 사전투표용지를 무단으로 인쇄할 수 있는 것으로 확인됐습니다.  일부 부재자 투표에서 암호 해독을 통해 특정 유권자의 기표 결과를 열람하거나, 전당대회 등 위탁 선거의 온라인투표시스템에선 대리 투표를 걸러낼 수 없는 문제점도 드러났습니다.  개표시스템 역시 보안관리가 미흡해 해커가 개표 결괏값을 변경할 수 있는 것으로 나타났다고 국정원은 설명했습니다.  국정원은 선관위가 이미 발생한 해킹 사고 대응에도 미흡했다고 지적했습니다.  선관위는 북한 소행의 해킹 사고에 대해 국정원 통보 전까지 사전 인지하지 못했습니다.  특히 2021년 4월에는 선관위의 인터넷 PC가 북한 ‘킴수키 조직'의 악성코드에 감염되어 메일함에 저장된 대외비 문건 등이 유출된 사실도 드러났습니다.  국정원은 다만 선관위 내부망에 대한 직접 해킹 사례는 확인되지 않았다고 밝혔습니다.  국정원은 합동 점검 결과 북한 등 외부세력이 의도할 경우 어느 때라도 선관위 시스템에 대한 공격이 가능한 상황이었다면서, 선관위와 함께 보완 조치에 나서겠다고 밝혔습니다.

주해토 대표 pc에 랜섬웨어 깔아야지

이 글 보고 얼마전에 DM으로 피싱사기 당할뻔한 ㅈ같은 기억 떠올라서 내용 정리해봄. 아마 걸렸으면 노트북 랜섬웨어 걸리고 벽돌됐을 듯  Arrington Capital이라는 Web3 투자 회사 사칭 사기였음  -어떤 일본 사람 이름 아이디로 팔로워 4천명 정도 달고 있는 쪽바리 새끼가 DM와서 내 플젝(Koreantickers) 에 관심있다며 접근함(꽤나 이메일스러운 어투로 DM을 보냄)  -정확히 뭘 원하는지 계속 말 안하고 질질 끌면서 진짜로 뭔가 협업/투자에 관심 있는 척 빌드업 함  -줌 미팅 하기로 하고 일정 잡음  -평일 저녁으로 하쟷더니 지들 바빠서 안된다고 주말저녁으로 잡음  -시간돼서 텔레그램 들어가서 그룹챗에 초대됐는데 쪽바리새끼 포함 한 5명 있었음  -헤드로 보이는 새끼가 뭐라뭐라 말하고 지들끼리 업무 관련 대화를  좀 하는 척 함  -화상회의 시작하려는데 지들이 중국에 있어서 Zoom이 안된다며 Tencent 화상회의 링크를 줌  -근데 링크가 tencent. 공식 서브도메인이 아니라 tencent-web 으로 시작하는 거임. -> 이때부터 약간 쎄했음  -링크로 들어갔더니 화상회의 앱을 다운받으라길래 다운받는데 뭔 용량이 ㅈㄴ크고 아무리봐도 텐센트 공식 앱이 아니길래 얼른 취소함  -계속 시간 끌면서 한국에서 보안상 이유로 안되는 척 했더니 웹 주소를 줌  -그 사이에 텔레그램 그룹에서는 지들 화상회의 참석해있는 캡처 보내고 왜안오냐고 존나 재촉함  -그룹에 있던 애 중 한 새끼가 나랑 똑같은 오류로 접속 안됐다가 다운 받고 됐다면서 왜 안되냐고 다운받으라고 계속 ㅈㄹ함 지능적인 새끼들  -웹으로 들어갔더니 뭔가 사람들 화면 속에서 움직이는게 보이는데 오디오 설정이 잘못됐다면서 화면 전체를 팝업이 덮어버리고, 무슨 명령어를 주더니 그대로 복사해서 파워쉘에 붙여넣으라고 안내함  -느낌이 존나 쎄해서 명령어 그대로 복사해서 코덱스한테 물어보니 외부 링크로 접속해서 강제로 뭔가를 다운받는 명령어라고 존나 위험해보인다고 함  -텔레그램 그룹방 들어가서 니들 진짜 내가 이 ㅈ같은 명령어 실행시킬 줄 알았냐고 ㅈㄹ함  -한 새끼가 그거 실행하면 된다고 I'm from Korea 라서 잘 안다고 개소리함  -욕 박고 그룹챗 나왔더니 바로 쪽바리가 차단박음  -진짜 내 플젝에 무슨 대단한 가치라도 있는 줄 알고(ㅈ밥인데)  거의 일주일을 어디 말도 못하고 혼자 설레다가 당한 거라 나름대로 심적으로 여파가 꽤 컸음  -분명히 그 새끼가 나 차단 박고 튀었는데 아직도 버젓이 애링턴 캐피탈 태그 달려있어서 혹시 내가 텐센트 시스템을 몰라서 뭔 기회를 걷어찬건가 하는 찝찝함이 아직도 남아있음  -사진은 피싱 사기꾼새끼 프로파일임
랜섬웨어 쇼핑좀 하러가볼까

갑자기 전쟁이 일어나서 은행의 데이터센터가 다 폭파되면 어떻게 될까요?  은행들은 과연 백업의 백업을 백업을 잘 해놨을까요?  옛날 전쟁이 일어나면 제일 먼저 챙기는것이 땅 문서 였습니다. 하지만 지금은 전부 전산화 되어있죠  데이터센터가 폭파되거나, 악성 랜섬웨어 한번 걸리면 영원히 찾을수 없는 데이터로요  비트코인은 전쟁으로 우리나라 전체가 잿더미가 되어도 네트워크와 데이터는 여전히 살아있습니다.  북한이 쳐들어와 서울이 함락되고 그 즉시 모든 예금을 무효화시켜도 우리는 옆나라로 도망쳐서 비트코인을 매도하여 살아가면 됩니다. (분명 그렇게 되면 비트코인을 받고 외국으로 도망치게 해주는 브로커들이 날뛸거라 생각합니다)  하지만 우리나라 예금이나, 주식을 가지고 있다면 다 무용지물이겠죠.  극단적 예시를 들어봤지만, 가장 위기때 빛나는 화폐가 바로 비트코인입니다.

攻勢防御で誰かまた外からパッチ宛ててくれんもんか。ルータを踏み台にされる怖さ、これはDDoSより厄介だ。AryStingerはRealtek RTL819X系の旧型ルータ少なくとも4,300台に感染し、D-Link DIR-850Lが約75%、CVE-2016-5681等でReconnaissance Proxy化している。EOL機器の棚卸し、ajb8.com系通信はISPなどでは要確認。エンドユーザのをテイクダウンさせるの難しいんだけどね。#セキュリティ is.gd/LX07Ar

To those unaware: I've been away from XIV for about 6 months, thank the DDOS attacks for that, and only just came back. Everything posted in that time has been things I've held in storage 😄 But, I did finally do a new pose so look forward to that at some point.

【AryStinger、旧式D-Linkルーター4,000台超を偵察・中継基盤化】  AryStingerという新たなボットネットが、古いD-Linkルーターなど4,000台超を侵害していると報じられました。  このマルウェアは感染機器を単なるDDoSノードではなく、スキャン、プロキシ、トンネリング、コマンド実行を行う「executor」として使います。攻撃者は分散した家庭用・小規模拠点ルーターを使い、本来の発信元を隠しながら後続侵入の偵察を進められます。  防御側はEOLルーターの交換、外部管理画面の遮断、`ajb8[.]com`や`dataexplore[.]cc`への通信、`/tmp/bin`配下の不審バイナリ、Dropbear SSHの不審起動を確認してください。  #AryStinger #DLink #IoTSecurity #Botnet #ThreatHunting #NetworkSecurity bleepingcomputer.com/news/se…

Lanzar acusaciones públicas sobre cambios en las direcciones IP de los servidores de la Registraduría y supuestas irregularidades en el software, justo en medio del preconteo electoral, es una estrategia irresponsable que busca deslegitimar los resultados de las urnas mediante el uso de tecnicismos informáticos alarmistas. En la arquitectura de redes, la variación de direcciones IP o el redireccionamiento de tráfico en servidores públicos no constituye de manera automática una vulneración o un hackeo; por el contrario, suele responder a procedimientos estándar de balanceo de cargas, mitigación de ataques cibernéticos (como los ataques DDoS) o configuraciones logísticas de contingencia implementadas por los administradores de los sistemas para asegurar la transmisión de datos durante picos masivos de tráfico. Sugerir que estos movimientos técnicos representan una alteración del censo o una manipulación de los votos es una ligereza que busca confundir a la opinión pública y crear un ambiente de sospecha generalizada. El sistema electoral colombiano cuenta con múltiples filtros de seguridad independientes y auditorías en las que participan ingenieros y delegados de todos los partidos políticos en competencia. Si existen pruebas reales y trazas técnicas verificables de un acceso no autorizado a las bases de datos de la Registraduría, el canal institucional obligatorio es presentarlas ante la Fiscalía General de la Nación y las autoridades judiciales competentes para que realicen un peritaje forense riguroso, y no utilizarlas como munición en redes sociales para sembrar caos institucional ante la inminencia de un desenlace electoral desfavorable para el oficialismo.

sample_tweets.text: 미국 연방 상원에서 ‘시진핑·중국공산당 규탄 결의안’이 만장일치로 통과했단다.  6월 16일 통과한 제444호 결의안에 그동안 중국공산당이 저지른 온갖 나쁜 짓이 구체적으로 적혀 있더라  *코로나19 사태와 관련한 국제사회 오도 *펜타닐 통제 실패와 WTO 가입 약속 위반 *대규모 간첩 활동과 사이버 공격 *대만 군사 압박과 남중국해 강압 행위 *러시아·이란·북한과의 밀착 *위그르 대규모 구금과 티베트 문화 탄압 *홍콩의 자유 억압과 종교 박해 *파룬궁 수련자 등 양심수 대상 강제 장기 적출  미국 상원 내부에도 중공이 매수한 의원이 있을낀데 이렇게 만장일치로 통과했다는 사실에 내는 좀 놀랬다.  미국 의회가 시진핑을 ‘독재자’로 명시했다는 점, 그라고 중국을 ‘명확한 적대 세력’으로 규정지은 이런 점도 정말 마음에 드네.  미국은 앞으로 걱정이 없것다. 정치인들이 바르게 보고 바르게 나아가고 있으니 말이다.  우리나라는 언제 바르게 될란지  #중국공산당 #시진핑 #미국상원 #결의안 nitter.privacyredirect.com/SaroViews/status/20686…

한미공조 수사가 아니라 국제 다국적 연합 수사가 필요한 사안입니다.  <출처. 구글 AI> 미국을 비롯한 피해국들이 합동 수사단을 구성해 대한민국 정부나 개입 기관, 관련자들을 수사할 가능성은 매우 높습니다.  실제 국제사회에서 러시아, 중국, 이란 등이 타국 선거에 개입했을 때 적용되었던 선례와 국제법적 절차를 바탕으로 볼 때, 가상의 상황에서 발생할 수 있는 구체적인 수사 가능성과 시나리오는 다음과 같습니다.  1. 다국적 합동 수사단(JIT) 구성 가능성 여러 국가가 동시에 동일한 주체(가상의 시나리오 속 한국)로부터 선거 개입 피해를 입었다면, 각국은 정보와 사법 역량을 결합합니다.  ◈ 증거 공유 미국, 영국, 캐나다 등 정보 동맹국들이 사이버 공격 기법(IP 주소, 악성코드 시그니처)과 자금 흐름을 공동 추적합니다.  ◈ 공동 수사 체계 유로폴(Europol)이나 파이브 아이즈(Five Eyes) 시스템을 기반으로 한 민관 합동 수사단이 조직되어 한국 내 특정 기관(예: 정보기관이나 군 사이버 부대)을 표적으로 수사를 전개합니다.

sample_texts: Anthropic이 KYC를 도입합니다. 그러면 Fable을 돌려받을 수 있을까요? 😁 KYC는 이론적으로 두 가지 우려를 해결할 수 있습니다. 위협 행위자들이 모델을 사이버 무기

sample_tweets.text: Anthropic이 KYC를 도입합니다. 그러면 Fable을 돌려받을 수 있을까요? 😁 KYC는 이론적으로 두 가지 우려를 해결할 수 있습니다. 위협 행위자들이 모델을 사이버 무기에 활용하는지 중국 AI 연구소들이 Fable을 증류(distillation)해 자체 모델을 만드는지 KYC는 마찰을 추가할 것입니다. 하지만 문제를 해결하지는 못합니다. 은행권은 50년간 KYC를 사용해왔지만 여전히 세탁 자금의 1% 미만만 적발합니다. 7월 8일부터 @AnthropicAI는 소비자 사용자(Free, Pro, Max)에게 특정 기능 이용 전 정부 발급 사진 ID와 실시간 셀피를 요구할 수 있습니다. 인증은 OpenAI가 제재 명단 대조 가입자 심사에 사용하는 것과 동일한 업체 @withpersona를 통해 운영됩니다. (Persona에게 큰 호재!) 미국 양대 선도 AI 연구소가 이제 금융권이 수십 년간 운영해온 동일한 컴플라이언스 인프라 위에 올라선 셈입니다. 저는 금융 서비스 분야에서 수년간 그 인프라를 가까이서 지켜봤습니다. 감시 패닉과 안전 자화자찬에서 벗어난 냉철한 시각: KYC는 애당초 진짜 문제가 아니었던 사람들에게 작동합니다. 미성년자, 일시적으로 제재된 자, 명백히 제재 대상인 자, 안일한 이용자를 걸러냅니다. 나름 유용하고, 오용의 비용을 소폭 높입니다. 하지만 막으려 한다고 내세우는 행위자에게는 거의 아무런 영향도 주지 못합니다. 정부 ID로는 API를 통해 모델을 증류하는 자를 막을 수 없습니다. 탈옥(jailbreak)도 막지 못합니다. 국가 행위자나 사기 조직은 은행 온보딩을 통과하는 것과 같은 방식으로 합성 또는 도용 서류를 들고 옵니다. OpenAI의 자체 개발자 포럼에는 조직 인증을 우회하는 방법이 공개되어 있습니다. 금융이 그 증거입니다. 지구상에서 신원 확인이 가장 철저한 산업, 모든 계좌가 감시 목록과 대조되고, 모든 결제가 모니터링됩니다. 그럼에도 세계는 여전히 매년 8000억~2조 달러를 그 시스템을 통해 세탁합니다. 1% 미만만 압수됩니다. TD Bank는 2024년 그것을 대규모로 방조한 대가로 기록적인 13억 달러의 벌금을 냈습니다. KYC는 그것을 결코 해결하지 못했습니다. 그것은 기본 조건(table stakes)이 되었습니다. 일반 사용자에게는 마찰, 제공자에게는 비용, 그리고 그것이 겨냥하는 실제 적에게는 미미한 효과. AI는 금융이 반세기에 걸쳐 걸어온 컴플라이언스 여정을 스피드런(speedrun)하고 있습니다. 신원 인증은 프론티어 AI 연구소들이 규제받는 금융 기관처럼 운영되는 방향으로 나아가는 첫 번째 가시적 단계입니다. 저는 향후 1년 내에 ID 확인이 모든 프론티어 연구소에서 기본이 될 것으로 예상합니다. 그리고 진짜 보안 싸움은 그것이 있어야 할 곳, 즉 모델 계층으로 이동할 것으로 봅니다. 그리고 잠재적으로 훨씬 더 많은 활용 가능성도 열립니다. 물건을 구매할 수 있는 에이전트 지갑과 인증 정보 AI 작업에 대한 사람의 '승인' 모든 것을 막지는 못할 수도 있습니다. 하지만 KYC에는 분명 나름의 용도가 있습니다.

sample_tweets.text: 일본 AI 스타트업, Claude의 Fable 5 및 Mythos와 대등한 성능의 모델 공개  Sakana AI는 두 가지 모델을 출시했습니다. Fugu: 빠르고 지연 시간이 짧으며 일상적인 코딩 및 채팅에 적합 Fugu Ultra: AI 연구, 논문 재현, 사이버 보안, 특허 검색 등 복잡한 다단계 문제에서 최고 수준의 품질 제공  도쿄에 본사를 둔 Sakana AI는 오늘날 거의 모든 AI 모델의 기반이 된 'Transformer' 논문의 공동 저자를 포함한 연구진이 설립한 기업입니다.  Sakana Fugu는 단일 API 엔드포인트 뒤에서 특화된 모델들을 자동으로 조율하여 마치 하나의 모델처럼 작동하는 멀티 에이전트 AI 시스템입니다.  Fugu Ultra는 공학, 과학, 추론 분야의 최고 난이도 벤치마크에서 Anthropic의 Fable 5 및 Mythos Preview와 대등한 성능을 보이며, 자동 연구(AutoResearch), 기계 설계, 재무 예측 등의 작업에서는 Gemini 3.1 Pro, Opus 4.8, GPT-5.5를 능가한다고 알려져 있습니다.

$ONDS   2026 FIFA 월드컵에서 이스라엘 방산, 보안 기술 기업들이 드론 위협과 사이버 위협에 대응하는 역할을 하고 있다는 소식입니다.  특히 $ONDS 의 자회사 @Sentrycs 가 월드컵 인프라에 C-UAS 솔루션을 배치한 사실이 언급되면서, $ONDS  투자자로서 흥미롭네요.  단순한 기술 공급이 아니라, 실제 대형 국제 행사의 보안으로 채택된 점이 매우 중요하죠.  대형 국제 행사 보안은 일회성 계약이 아니라, 유지보수와 업그레이드 및 추가 확장으로 이어질 가능성이 높습니다.  특히 Cyberhawk 인수와 결합하면, 검사와 C-UAS를 연계한 통합 솔루션 제공이 가능해질 수 있죠.  또한 $ONDS 가 강조하는 LADOS가 @Sentrycs 와 결합하면, 단순 탐지나 요격을 넘어 통합 관제까지 가능한 $ONDS 가 추구하는 System of Systems 전략의 실전 사례가 될 수 있죠.  2026 월드컵 기간에 부여된 목표를 성공적으로 수행한다면, 다른 대형 이벤트로 확산될수있습니다.   그렇게 된다면 $ONDS 의 C-UAS 사업에 엄청난 성장 동력이 될 수 있습니다.  $ONDS 의 성공적인 실제 운영 결과를 기대해봐야죠. 🙏

1페이지 요약  Jason P. Lowery의 MIT 석사논문 Softwar: A Novel Theory on Power Projection and the National Strategic Significance of Bitcoin은 비트코인을 단순한 화폐나 투자자산이 아니라, “사이버 공간에서 물리적 비용을 부과하는 새로운 권력 투사 기술”로 해석해야 한다고 주장한다. 저자는 현재 비트코인 논의가 금융, 통화, 경제 이론에 지나치게 갇혀 있어 국가안보적 의미를 놓칠 수 있다고 본다.  논문의 핵심 개념은 “Power Projection Theory”다. 자연계와 인간 사회에서 자원 소유권은 결국 물리적 힘을 투사하고 방어할 수 있는 능력과 연결되어 왔다는 것이다. 군대가 육상, 해상, 공중, 우주라는 통로에서 자유로운 접근과 교역을 보장하듯, 21세기의 핵심 통로인 사이버 공간에서도 중요한 정보 비트를 보호하려면 물리적 비용을 부과하는 방식이 필요하다고 저자는 말한다.  저자는 비트코인의 작업증명, 즉 Proof-of-Work를 이런 관점에서 재해석한다. 일반 소프트웨어는 논리적 규칙으로만 컴퓨터를 제약하지만, 비트코인은 전력, 장비, 해시 연산이라는 실제 물리적 비용을 요구한다. 따라서 공격자나 악의적 행위자가 네트워크를 조작하려면 막대한 현실 세계의 에너지 비용을 감수해야 한다. 이 점에서 비트코인은 “전자-사이버 보안 시스템”이며, 저자는 이를 “softwar”라고 부른다.  논문은 비트코인의 전략적 중요성이 금융 결제보다 더 클 수 있다고 주장한다. 비트코인은 가치 있는 금융 정보뿐 아니라, 원칙적으로 모든 종류의 디지털 정보를 물리적으로 보호할 수 있는 새로운 보안 패러다임의 실증 사례라는 것이다. 저자는 이를 “planetary state mechanism”, 즉 지구 규모의 상태 기계로도 설명한다. 비트코인이 가장 강력한 작업증명 네트워크가 된 이상, 다른 유사 시스템은 같은 수준의 물리적 보안성과 희소성을 재현하기 어렵다고 본다.  정책적으로 저자는 미국이 비트코인을 단순히 암호화폐로만 규제하거나 금융 전문가에게만 해석을 맡기는 것은 위험하다고 경고한다. 비트코인이 실제로 새로운 권력 투사 기술이라면, 이를 조기에 이해하고 채택하는 국가가 사이버 공간에서 전략적 우위를 얻을 수 있기 때문이다. 그래서 저자는 비트코인 비축, 채굴 산업 보호, 관련 연구 확대, 군사·사이버보안 관점의 정책 검토를 제안한다.  다만 이 논문은 실증 검증보다는 grounded theory 방식으로 새로운 이론적 틀을 제시하는 작업이다. 저자도 자신의 주장이 확정된 결론이 아니라 후속 연구로 검증되어야 할 가설임을 인정한다. 요약하면, 이 논문의 메시지는 “비트코인의 본질은 돈이 아니라 권력 균형과 사이버 공간의 물리적 보안일 수 있다”는 것이다.

📋 분석 요약  데이터의 중요성과 함께 기술의 통제권, 즉 '기술 주권'의 필요성이 대두되었습니다. 특히 특정 국가에 대한 기술 의존도가 높아질 경우, 정치적, 경제적으로 취약해질 수 있다는 인식이 확산되면서 각국은 자체적인 기술 역량을 키우고 관련 공급망을 안정화하려는 노력을 기울이고 있습니다. 이는 마치 반도체를 둘러싼 국가 간 경쟁처럼, 앞으로 더 많은 기술 분야에서 벌어질 경쟁의 예고편이라고 볼 수 있습니다. Clean Election Watch 시각호주는 지리적으로 고립되어 있고, 상당수의 첨단 기술을 해외에 의존하고 있는 상황입니다. 이러한 상황에서 기술 주권을 논하는 것은 호주에게 더욱 시급하고 중요한 과제입니다. 단순히 해외 기술을 도입하는 것을 넘어, 호주만의 강점을 살려 혁신적인 기술을 개발하고, 이를 통해 경제적 이익을 창출하는 선순환 구조를 만드는 것이 중요합니다. 예를 들어, 호주는 청정 에너지 분야에서 잠재력이 크므로, 이와 관련된 기술 개발에 집중하여 글로벌 시장을 선도하는 것도 좋은 전략이 될 수 있습니다. 또한, 강력한 사이버 보안 시스템 구축은 디지털 시대의 필수적인 안전 장치로서, 호주 국민의 개인 정보와 국가 기밀을 보호하는 데 핵심적인 역할을 할 것입니다.기술 주권 확보…

충격) 비트코인 이더리움은 구식 쓰레기 -프랑스 정부   🇫🇷 한 정부가 비트코인의 암호화를 구식으로 선언했습니다  프랑스의 사이버 기관은 2027년 이후 양자 안전 암호화가 없는 어떤 보안 제품도 인증하지 않을 것입니다.  암호화폐를 한 번도 언급하지 않습니다.  하지만 비트코인과 이더리움은 프랑스가 방금 거부한 정확히 그 알고리즘으로 작동합니다.  타원 곡선 암호화.  "지금 수확하고, 나중에 해독하기"에 대한 시계가 더 크게 똑딱거리기 시작했습니다.

#ThreatHunting  Open Directory (Index of /) -> 101.201.62[.]40  Process: - All files in the directory (.exe, .part, .reg, .bat, .sh) were examined. - Static and dynamic analysis was applied to suspicious samples. - Code segments were evaluated for common origin detection using SHA-256 comparison.  Static and Dynamic Analysis Findings: - The PE code sections (CODE sections) of the four different samples presented as HWID spoofer, Steam installation, motherboard replacement tool, and Windows activator have the same SHA-256 hash value (9251b16208...). The fact that only the .rsrc sections differ indicates that the core code is common, but the embedded payloads have been modified. - The detected mutexes, SMTP configurations, and C2 indicators show similarities to the previously reported XRed/XRedRAT infrastructure. During the analysis, functions related to keyboard logging, SMTP communication, and remote command execution were detected. - The SteamSetup_iELl2y3l_.exe.part instance does not have a valid Valve digital signature and shares the same code section as the other three instances. - Hiding -> High entropy of 7.75–7.96 observed in the .rsrc partition (indicator of compressed or encrypted content); Synaptics.exe is silently writing to disk within the NSIS installation structure.  In Summary: At first glance, the package appeared to be a game cheat/activator archive, but analysis of the files revealed the true picture;  Key Components: - Backdoor: QTspoofer硬刷工具.exe, SteamSetup_iELl2y3l_.exe.part, 一键主板修改_微星无脑改_.exe, 激活系统.exe — XRed/XRedRAT similarity. - 关杀毒.exe(.part) -> UPX packed; it includes functions designed to disable security products. - 大志无敌解码.exe -> It includes functions for modifying Windows Defender configurations. - 注册表管理.exe -> Dropper carrying 9 MB encrypted payload. - 重装工具_密码vip0.EXE -> It includes password protection, driver usage, encryption, and anti-debug techniques. - 请打开我-关闭管理员权限.reg -> EnableLUA=0 modifies the UAC configuration. - 修改显示器.exe -> EDID changers can be used for hardware identity manipulation purposes.  #IOCs:  - C2 / Network: xred.mooo[.]com, xred.site50[.]net, freedns.afraid[.]org, api.ilanzou[.]com, share.weiyun[.]com, xyboot[.]com  - E-mail / Extraction: xredline1[@]gmail.com, xredline2[@]gmail.com, xredline3[@]gmail.com  - Mutex: Synaptics2X  - Extracted Files: Synaptics.exe Synaptics.dll SUpdate.ini SSLLibrary.dll cachex.ini autorun.inf  - Registry / Persistence: [HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System] "EnableLUA"=dword:00000000 [HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System]  reg add "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender" /v "DisableAntiSpyware" /d 1 /t REG_DWORD /f reg add "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender\Real-Time Protection" /v "DisableBehaviorMonitoring" /d 1 /t REG_DWORD /f reg add "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender\Real-Time Protection" /v "DisableIOAVProtection" /d 1 /t REG_DWORD /f reg add "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender\Real-Time Protection" /v "DisableOnAccessProtection" /d 1 /t REG_DWORD /f reg add "HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows Defender\Real-Time Protection" /v "DisableRealtimeMonitoring" /d 1 /t REG_DWORD /f  #ThreatIntelligence #DFIR #MalwareAnalysis #OpenDirectory #OSINT #BlueTeam #CyberSecurity #Cyberthint

自分用に調べたので、日本のCSIRTメンバーに共有  - mukul975 氏作のサイバーセキュリティAIスキル集。754個のSOC/DFIR/脅威インテリ実務手順書(SKILL.md)＋1,041本の参照用Pythonヘルパーで構成 - MITRE ATT&CK / NIST CSF など主要5フレームワークにマッピング、Claude Code等で利用可能  【公式性・信頼性】 - Anthropic公式ではない（名前にAnthropicが入るが README に「Not affiliated with Anthropic PBC」と明記。詐称ではなくコミュニティプロジェクトと正直に表示） - スター17.1k / Fork 2.1k / Apache-2.0 / 直近も活発に更新。作者は2018年からのアカウントでセキュリティ専攻、一定の信頼性あり - 軽セキュリティ監査では悪性コード（任意実行・難読化・認証情報窃取・外部送信）は未検出。スクリプトは自動実行ではなく参照素材  【既存スキルとの重複・差分】 - ほぼ重複なし。既存の security-audit / vuln-scan / threat-model 等は「自分のコードを監査する」プロセス系 - 本リポジトリはマルウェア解析・脅威ハンティング等の実務ドメイン知識系で、用途は補完的  【導入是非の結論】条件付き推奨（フルコピーは非推奨、選択導入を推奨） - 理由1: 754スキル全部の導入はコンテキスト肥大・トークン浪費。通常業務とは大半が無関係 - 理由2: ただしSOC/DFIR実務が発生した時の参照知識としては価値が高く品質も良い - 推奨運用: git clone で参照用に置き、必要な数スキルだけ手元の skills/ に取り込む。導入前に対象スクリプトを必ず目視確認（非公式のため自己責任）

📢선관위 서버는 언제, 포렌식?🫆🔎  🎯제대로 수사 할까?  합수본은 11일 경기 과천시의 중앙선관위와 서울시선관위, 투표용지 부족 사태가 발생한 송파·강남·서초·광진·동작구선관위

sample_tweets.text: 📢선관위 서버는 언제, 포렌식?🫆🔎  🎯제대로 수사 할까?  합수본은 11일 경기 과천시의 중앙선관위와 서울시선관위, 투표용지 부족 사태가 발생한 송파·강남·서초·광진·동작구선관위 등 7곳에 대한 압수수색에 나섰다. 이번 압수수색 대상에는 선관위 서버 등 전산 시스템도 포함됐다.   다만🔎 이는 직원들의 [대화 내역 등]을 확인하기 위해 수사당국이 원격 접근을 하는 방식으로, 실제 서버를 물리적으로 확보한 것은 아닌 것⁉️으로 전해졌다.  🌋당연히 대화 보다 중요한 것이, 통합선거인 명부 관리 시스템과 로그기록들인데 핵심 로데이터 포렌식은 쏙 빼고 뭘 본다는 건지?  🔎🎯📢 잘지켜 봐야 한다.  지난 윤대통령 탄핵심판때에도 헌재는 서버 및 투표관련 자료 검증을 무려 3번이나 기각시켰다 [노코멘트 무시]. 심지어 선관위 사무총장이 협력하겠다 공언까지 했었다. 당시에도, '도대체 뭘 감추어야 할 것이 있길래 겨우 서버 디지털 포렌식인데 저렇게 막나' 하고 의구심을 가졌다.  그런데 탄핵 심판 증인으로 나온 국정원3차장이 2023년 당시 점검때도 상당히 비협조적이어서 겨우 6,400대의 컴퓨터 중 겨우 317대(5%)진단했는데 31.5점 이었고 (110개 국가기관 평균-81.9점/최하 44.6점)-추가 진행했다면 더 심각 했을것이라는 충격적 증언을 듣고서야 이해가 되었다. 👀 심지어 점검하면서 느낀점은 국가위기가 초래할만큼 잠재적으로 위험한 상태였다는 암시적 멘트까지 남겼다. 선관위가 개방해줘서 점검가능한 것이라고 주장한부분에 거의 코웃음치다 시피했다. 모의테스트때 쉽게 뚫렸단다..북한은 뭐..(당시 해커 그룹 킴수키가 포함된 북한 총국 산하 라자루스는 이미 최고 수준 해커그룹이다- 비트코인 최대 탈취)  🤔 아무튼 분명 국정원 점검 이후, 다양한 방법을 통해 보완한 것으로 알고 있다. 그런데, 이번 선관위 직원의 폭로까지 비추어 보았을때, 눈가리고 아웅식으로 전산조작 가능한 핵심 부분은 그대로 존치되어 유지된 것으로 추정된다. 계속 이런식이다.  국방과 마찬가지다. 이건 좌우의 문제가 아닌 아래위 문제다. 도대체 어디까지 뚫려 있는 건가?  📢이번에도 하는척으로, 마무리 되어선 안된다.  절대로.🇰🇷🇺🇸

📢선관위 서버는 언제, 포렌식?🫆🔎  🎯제대로 수사 할까?  합수본은 11일 경기 과천시의 중앙선관위와 서울시선관위, 투표용지 부족 사태가 발생한 송파·강남·서초·광진·동작구선관위 등 7곳에 대한 압수수색에 나섰다. 이번 압수수색 대상에는 선관위 서버 등 전산 시스템도 포함됐다.   다만🔎 이는 직원들의 [대화 내역 등]을 확인하기 위해 수사당국이 원격 접근을 하는 방식으로, 실제 서버를 물리적으로 확보한 것은 아닌 것⁉️으로 전해졌다.  🌋당연히 대화 보다 중요한 것이, 통합선거인 명부 관리 시스템과 로그기록들인데 핵심 로데이터 포렌식은 쏙 빼고 뭘 본다는 건지?  🔎🎯📢 잘지켜 봐야 한다.  지난 윤대통령 탄핵심판때에도 헌재는 서버 및 투표관련 자료 검증을 무려 3번이나 기각시켰다 [노코멘트 무시]. 심지어 선관위 사무총장이 협력하겠다 공언까지 했었다. 당시에도, '도대체 뭘 감추어야 할 것이 있길래 겨우 서버 디지털 포렌식인데 저렇게 막나' 하고 의구심을 가졌다.  그런데 탄핵 심판 증인으로 나온 국정원3차장이 2023년 당시 점검때도 상당히 비협조적이어서 겨우 6,400대의 컴퓨터 중 겨우 317대(5%)진단했는데 31.5점 이었고 (110개 국가기관 평균-81.9점/최하 44.6점)-추가 진행했다면 더 심각 했을것이라는 충격적 증언을 듣고서야 이해가 되었다. 👀 심지어 점검하면서 느낀점은 국가위기가 초래할만큼 잠재적으로 위험한 상태였다는 암시적 멘트까지 남겼다. 선관위가 개방해줘서 점검가능한 것이라고 주장한부분에 거의 코웃음치다 시피했다. 모의테스트때 쉽게 뚫렸단다..북한은 뭐..(당시 해커 그룹 킴수키가 포함된 북한 총국 산하 라자루스는 이미 최고 수준 해커그룹이다- 비트코인 최대 탈취)  🤔 아무튼 분명 국정원 점검 이후, 다양한 방법을 통해 보완한 것으로 알고 있다. 그런데, 이번 선관위 직원의 폭로까지 비추어 보았을때, 눈가리고 아웅식으로 전산조작 가능한 핵심 부분은 그대로 존치되어 유지된 것으로 추정된다. 계속 이런식이다.  국방과 마찬가지다. 이건 좌우의 문제가 아닌 아래위 문제다. 도대체 어디까지 뚫려 있는 건가?  📢이번에도 하는척으로, 마무리 되어선 안된다.  절대로.🇰🇷🇺🇸

sample_tweets.text: AI is helping SOC teams triage alerts faster, investigate incidents more efficiently, and automate time-consuming tasks.  But there's a catch.  AI can accelerate investigations. It can't replace the skills needed to validate findings, understand business impact, and make decisions when situations become complex.  The organisations seeing the greatest return on AI aren't just investing in tools. They're investing in analysts with strong foundations in threat detection, investigation, and incident response.  Because AI is only as effective as the people using it.  👉 Read our latest blog to learn how to build an AI-ready SOC team: centri.org/blog/posts/ai-is-…

Here's the tell in Jennifer Moore's career:  Special Agent in Charge of the Intelligence and Incident Response Division at the Washington Field Office and Assistant Director of the Security Division.  You can't do that job without massive interaction with the CIA, daily. And surprise, she went to work for General Electric (Aerospace, South) meaning South American/Latin America. You can't make this up.

📢 RCDS has published its third monthly monitoring report on hate speech targeting Nagorno-Karabakh Armenians during Armenia’s parliamentary election campaign.  The report documents an unprecedented escalation of hostile rhetoric during the official campaign period. The report finds that representatives of the ruling party, led by Prime Minister Nikol Pashinyan, played a central role in promoting narratives that collectively blame Nagorno-Karabakh Armenians for the loss of Nagorno-Karabakh and portray them as deserters.  Particular attention is given to the May 18 incident involving Nagorno-Karabakh activist Arthur Osipyan, during which Pashinyan told him that he “should have gone and died instead of our children” and repeatedly asked, “Why are you alive?” The report argues that such rhetoric stigmatizes an entire community by implying that Nagorno-Karabakh Armenians who survived the war and raise political demands are somehow less deserving of life than those who died. It further notes that these statements form part of a broader narrative that shifts responsibility for ethnic cleansing onto its victims.  The report also documents how pro-government influencers amplified and radicalized these messages online through dehumanizing language, collective blame, and calls to expel Nagorno-Karabakh Armenians from Armenia. It additionally highlights cases where state assistance to displaced persons was framed as a source of political indebtedness and loyalty.  The monitoring concludes that law enforcement agencies have remained largely inactive in response to these incidents, contributing to an environment in which hate speech continues to spread unchecked.  This report was produced with the financial support of the European Union and the German Marshall Fund of the United States – Transatlantic Foundation (GMF TF). Its contents are the sole responsibility of the Regional Center for Democracy and Security and do not necessarily reflect the views of the European Union or the GMF TF. May 19, 2014. The Justice Department unsealed an indictment naming five officers of the People's Liberation Army, and for about 48 hours the world briefly paid attention to something that had been happening for years.  The five defendants: Wang Dong, Sun Kailiang, Wen Xinyu, Huang Zhenyu, and Gu Chunhui. All officers in PLA Unit 61398, based in Shanghai. Cybersecurity researchers had been writing about Unit 61398 for years at that point. Mandiant had published a detailed report on the unit in 2013. The hacking wasn't exactly a secret. What was new was the United States government putting specific names in a federal indictment and calling it what it was: economic espionage, prosecutable under U.S. law, committed by specific human beings who theoretically could be arrested.  "Theoretically" is doing a lot of work in that sentence.  The charges themselves were serious and specific. The DOJ documented a systematic campaign across nuclear power, steel, and solar energy. Westinghouse Electric was targeted during active negotiations with Chinese state-owned enterprises over nuclear power plant construction. Let that framing sit for a moment, but in a different way than the cliché: the Chinese counterparts in those negotiations had access to Westinghouse's technical specifications, design documents, negotiating strategies, and business plans. They were negotiating against a company while holding that company's hand. U.S. Steel and Allegheny Technologies had manufacturing processes and competitive positioning data stolen, directly benefiting Chinese state-owned steel producers. SolarWorld AG lost research, manufacturing processes, and business strategies while simultaneously being undercut by Chinese solar manufacturers in trade disputes.  The United Steelworkers union was also targeted. The union's confidential communications about ongoing trade negotiations with Chinese counterparts were stolen, meaning Beijing had advance knowledge of American positions before those talks happened. This is worth dwelling on: not just corporate IP, but labor union strategy in trade fights with China. The breadth of collection reflects actual intelligence priorities, not random opportunism.  The methodology was professional and patient. Spear-phishing for initial access, custom malware for persistence, deliberate reconnaissance to identify specific valuable material before exfiltration. This wasn't smash-and-grab. The tradecraft reflected the collection requirements of a military intelligence apparatus with specific economic tasking. Get the nuclear specs. Get the steel processes. Get the negotiating positions. Get out without tripping alarms for as long as possible.  The DOJ called this "the first ever charges against a state actor" for cyber economic espionage. That framing mattered. By naming individuals rather than just attributing attacks to "China" or "state-sponsored actors," the indictment created a legal hook. If any of the five ever traveled to a country with a U.S. extradition treaty, they could theoretically be arrested. The practical likelihood of that happening was approximately zero, and everyone involved knew it. But the indictment wasn't primarily about getting these five men into a courtroom. It was about establishing that the United States considered this criminal behavior, full stop, subject to individual accountability, and that the government had both the capability and the willingness to make the case publicly with specific detail.  The public attribution dimension was genuinely significant. The indictment gave the business community, the cybersecurity industry, and international partners an unprecedented level of specificity about PLA operations. This is how it was done. These are the tools. These are the targets. These are the people. For companies trying to defend themselves, for allies trying to calibrate their own posture toward Chinese cyber activity, that kind of detailed public record had real value beyond its symbolic content.  China's response was predictably furious and only moderately convincing. Beijing called the prosecution hypocritical in light of the Snowden disclosures, which had revealed extensive U.S. intelligence collection. This argument has a certain surface logic that collapses under examination. The distinction the DOJ was drawing, and it's a real distinction, is between national security intelligence collection and economic espionage for commercial benefit. Stealing another country's military plans is one category of activity. Stealing Westinghouse's nuclear designs and handing them to competing Chinese state enterprises so they can undercut Westinghouse in commercial markets is a different category. Governments have always spied on each other for strategic purposes. Using military intelligence assets to systematically loot foreign companies for the benefit of your domestic commercial competitors sits in genuinely different territory, both legally and normatively. Beijing knew this, which is why the Snowden deflection was deployed so quickly.  China also suspended participation in a bilateral cybersecurity working group that had been established to manage tensions between the two countries. This was the retaliation available and they took it. The diplomatic fallout illustrated the core tension in using criminal prosecution against state actors as a counter-espionage tool: you get the attribution, you get the legal precedent, you get the public record, and you also get a diplomatic incident that complicates other things you're trying to do with the same country. There's no clean resolution to that tension. It's a genuine tradeoff, and the Obama administration made a judgment that the costs were worth the precedent.  Whether the deterrence logic actually worked is a more complicated question. The five defendants never faced trial. Unit 61398 did not close up shop. Chinese economic cyber espionage against American companies continued after May 2014. The 2015 Obama-Xi agreement, under which China nominally committed to stopping cyber-enabled commercial theft, came later and was attributed to a combination of diplomatic pressure, the indictments, and the threat of economic sanctions. How much weight the indictments carried in producing that agreement is genuinely unclear.  What the 2014 indictment did establish, durably, was a template. The DOJ would use it again. Named individuals. Specific charges. Detailed public attribution. Permanent legal jeopardy attached to specific human beings, even if no extradition is expected. The framework built in May 2014 became the standard approach for publicly confronting state-sponsored cyber intrusions, applied subsequently to Iranian hackers, North Korean operators, Russian GRU officers.  The five men named in that indictment are presumably still in China, presumably still working, presumably aware that their names are in a U.S. federal court record that doesn't expire. That's a strange kind of accountability. It's also probably the only kind available, and the DOJ made a reasonable bet that having it was better than not having it.  foreigninterference.org/post… #foreigninterference #CyberEspionage #AdvancedPersistentThreatOperations #TradeSecretTheft #PersistentNetworkInfiltration #MalwareDistribution #AssetRecruitment

The Namibia Cyber Security Incident Response Team (NAM-CSIRT) has identified 13 Namibian organisations potentially exposed to a global cyber security incident that could allow hackers to gain unauthorised access to corporate networks, steal sensitive information and launch ransomware attacks.  The incident, known as FortiBleed, involves the exposure of administrator credentials, virtual private network (VPN) credentials and firewall configuration data linked to internet-facing Fortinet FortiGate devices used by organisations worldwide.  NAM-CSIRT, which operates under the Communications Regulatory Authority of Namibia (CRAN), said all affected Namibian organisations have been contacted directly and provided with guidance on urgent remedial measures to secure their systems and reduce the risk of compromise.  According to NAM-CSIRT, the exposed credentials could enable cyber criminals to bypass security controls, impersonate legitimate users, alter network settings and potentially gain access to sensitive corporate information. In severe cases, the vulnerability could be exploited to facilitate ransomware attacks.  thebrief.com.na/2026/06/13-n… #namibia #cybersecurity #ransomware #organisations @MICTNamibia @CRANamibia @EmmaTheofelus

NIGERIAN FIRE SERVICE EQUIPS ENUGU BATCH B STREAM I CORPS MEMBERS WITH CRITICAL FIRE SAFETY AND EMERGENCY RESPONSE SKILLS AT AWGU ORIENTATION CAMP  As part of ongoing efforts to equip Corps Members with essential life-saving skills and enhance their preparedness for emergencies, the Nigerian Fire Service conducted a comprehensive fire safety and emergency response training for 2026 Batch B Stream I Corps Members at the NYSC Permanent Orientation Camp, Awgu, Enugu State.   The training featured practical and theoretical sessions on fire prevention, firefighting techniques, emergency evacuation procedures, fire extinguisher usage, fire suppression methods, and the importance of personal protective equipment. Through interactive discussions, live demonstrations, and expert guidance, corps members gained valuable knowledge on preventing fire outbreaks, responding effectively to emergencies, and promoting a culture of safety within their communities and places of assignment.  The lecture was delivered by DSF Chude Gerald of the Enugu Fire Department and was specifically designed to equip Corps Members with practical knowledge and skills that could help prevent fire incidents, minimize casualties during emergencies, and encourage safety-conscious behaviour both during and after the Orientation Course.  Addressing the Corps Members, Mr. Gerald defined fire as a rapid chemical reaction that produces heat and light through the process of combustion. He explained that fire cannot exist without the simultaneous presence of oxygen, heat, and fuel, collectively known as the Fire Triangle. According to him, modern fire science introduces a fourth element known as the chemical chain reaction, thereby forming the Fire Tetrahedron. He emphasized that understanding these components is fundamental to effective firefighting, as extinguishing a fire requires the removal of at least one of these essential elements.  The lecturer further educated Corps Members on the various classes of fire and the importance of correctly identifying the nature of a fire before attempting to extinguish it. He explained that each category of fire requires specific extinguishing agents and cautioned that the use of inappropriate firefighting methods could aggravate an emergency. He particularly warned against the use of water on electrical fires due to the risk of electrocution and the potential for further damage.  Speaking on firefighting techniques, Mr. Gerald explained that fire suppression can be achieved through cooling, starvation, smothering, and interruption of the chemical chain reaction. He noted that water extinguishes fire by reducing heat, while Carbon Dioxide and foam extinguishers suppress flames by cutting off the oxygen supply. Dry Chemical Powder extinguishers, he added, are particularly effective because they disrupt the chemical reactions that sustain combustion.  A major aspect of the lecture focused on the various types of fire extinguishers and their specific applications. Corps Members were enlightened on the uses of Dry Chemical Powder, Carbon Dioxide, foam, and wet chemical extinguishers, as well as the circumstances under which each should be deployed. Corps members were encouraged to familiarize themselves with firefighting equipment available in their homes, workplaces, and public facilities to improve their readiness during emergencies..  During the practical segment of the training, officials of the Nigerian Fire Service conducted a live demonstration on the proper use of a fire extinguisher using a controlled fire outbreak. Corps Members were taught the internationally recognized PASS technique, which involves pulling the safety pin, aiming at the base of the fire, squeezing the handle, and sweeping the nozzle from side to side until the flames are extinguished. Participants were equally advised to ensure that a clear escape route is always maintained when attempting to combat a minor fire incident.

Claude Code creator gave a 24-minute prompting workshop  "Anthropic technical onboarding used to take 2-3 weeks"  Boris says Claude Code Q&A brought it down to about 2-3 days  The order he teaches:  1. Start with codebase Q&A  Before edits, make Claude explain the repo  Ask: - where is auth handled - how is this class used - how do I instantiate this object - why does this function have 15 arguments - look through Git history and explain why this changed  Claude reads files, follows examples, checks Git history, follows linked issues, and explains why the code exists  2. Make it plan before it edits  Use this prompt:  "Before you write code, make a plan Brainstorm options Name the files you need Ask for approval before changing anything"  Claude should explore first, plan second, edit third  3. Give it a way to check its work  A coding agent improves when it can see the result  Give it: - unit tests - integration tests - Puppeteer screenshots - iOS simulator screenshots - lint and typecheck commands  Prompt it like this:  "Build this UI Run it in the browser Take a screenshot Compare it to the mock Fix the differences Stop after 3 passes"  4. Put repo context in `CLAUDE.md`  Boris says `CLAUDE.md` is loaded at the start of every session  Put in: - test commands - build commands - style guide - common MCP tools - important files - architecture decisions - repo-specific gotchas  Keep it short  Long context burns the session before the work starts  5. Split shared and personal context  Shared repo rules go in project `CLAUDE.md`  Personal preferences stay local  Nested `CLAUDE.md` files can live inside child folders, so Claude pulls in folder-specific rules only when it works there  6. Turn repeated work into slash commands  Use `.claude/commands` for repo tasks you repeat  Examples: - label GitHub issues - generate release notes - check a PR - run the project test sequence - prepare a weekly shipped summary  7. Use shortcuts while it runs  - type `#` to make Claude remember a rule and fold it into `CLAUDE.md` - type `!` to run a shell command and add the output to context - hit `Esc` when an edit is almost right and one line needs changing - hit `Esc` twice to jump back in history - hit `Ctrl+R` to see the same output Claude sees  8. Use `claude -p` for scripts  For CI, incident response, or recurring checks:  - pass a prompt - allow specific tools - request JSON or streaming JSON - pipe into it and out of it like a Unix utility

The term "criticality" has a clear meaning in the realm of science. However, in the world of politics, its boundaries are ambiguous. Where the criticality begins can only be defined retrospectively. In other words, we always recognize a "state that was already critical" only after the fact.  More people than usual gathered in the morning conference room. No formal declaration of crisis had been issued. Yet, the atmosphere was clearly different. It wasn't the density of information that had increased, but rather the "compression rate" of information.  Southern Lebanon, northern Israel, and the Gulf region—multiple hotspots were simultaneously experiencing subtle fluctuations. Individually, these were small events, but their simultaneity changed their meaning.  I called this state structural resonance.  During the morning briefing, a military analyst showed a map. Lines were drawn, but they were not boundaries; they were merely symbols for observational convenience. In the actual field, the lines had already lost their meaning.  "The problem isn't location, it's interconnectedness."  Those words summarized everything.  The problem isn't individual conflicts, but rather the "structure of simultaneous reactions" between multiple small-scale actions. This is a sign of impending criticality.  In the afternoon, a report came in from the intelligence team. Discourse on social media, fragments of military actions, political statements—while seemingly independent, they are beginning to synchronize in a certain pattern.  I see a dangerous sign there.  When a system collapses, it first synchronizes.  Synchronization isn't order; rather, it's an alignment of instability.  In the evening, the US internal analysis was shared. "The current situation isn't an escalation, but a state of approaching a threshold." This expression was cautious, but the meaning was clear. It hasn't been crossed yet. But the conditions for crossing it are already falling into place.  The important thing is that no one can pinpoint that "moment of crossing."  The Israeli side's statements remain unchanged: "A preventative response to a threat." This phrase has been repeated before, but now it sounds slightly different. Because the boundary between prevention and response is becoming blurred.  I write in my notebook.  "Prevention is the act of bringing future conflicts into the present."  At that moment, the core of this chapter takes shape.  At night, short fragments arrive from multiple sources. None of them are confirmed information. Rather, they are unorganized signs. But that uncertainty is precisely what is important.  Confirmed information is too late. Signs are too early.  Only the "compressed uncertainty" in between is what drives reality forward.  Looking out the window, the city was quiet. But that quietness was not equilibrium. Multiple forces were simultaneously pulling at each other, yet not yet broken.

SECURITY INTELLIGENCE REPORT Report ID: INT‑CT‑BALOCH‑2026‑0622 Date: 22 June 2026 Subject: Allegation: RAW Directed BLA to Launch Major Attacks in Balochistan 1. Intelligence Overview  According to credible intelligence reports, India’s RAW has allegedly issued directives to the BLA (Baloch Liberation Army) to carry out large-scale terrorist attacks across Balochistan province. This move is claimed to be a direct retaliation following the recent court verdict sentencing Mahrang Baloch to life imprisonment for her role in violent incidents during the Gwadar Raji Muchi protest.  2. Alleged Plans & Objectives  The reported instructions include:  • Targeting security forces, military checkpoints, FC posts and police installations  • Attacking strategic infrastructure, energy projects and government facilities  • Creating widespread chaos, destabilizing the province and disrupting economic activity  • Using propaganda to damage Pakistan’s image locally and internationally  3. Background & Motivations  Intelligence assessments indicate long-standing links between RAW and militant groups in Balochistan. The alleged plot is seen as an attempt to:  • Avenge the conviction of Mahrang Baloch  • Undermine Pakistan’s sovereignty and development projects like CPEC  • Divert security resources and maintain unrest in the region  4. Current Status & Security Response  • Security and intelligence agencies have been placed on high alert across Balochistan.  • Enhanced surveillance, patrolling and security measures have been activated to prevent planned attacks.  • Investigations are ongoing to verify the authenticity of the intelligence and track the movement of militant elements.  5. Conclusion  This reported directive reflects an attempt to escalate cross-border proxy activity. While the intelligence is being closely monitored, security forces remain prepared to counter any threat and ensure the safety of citizens and state installations. Further updates will be shared as investigations progress. Distribution: CTD Balochistan, Corps Headquarters Quetta

The Fivecast Tradecraft Team just published a practitioner-level breakdown of how modern foreign interference actually works, and the more useful conversation is about what the trends in it point toward.  The definitional split they draw between espionage and interference is the right starting point. Classic espionage has a recognizable shape: a foreign intelligence officer, a government employee with a clearance, some form of recruitment or theft. Counterintelligence agencies spent decades building machinery to catch exactly that. The problem is that the threat has sprawled well past those boundaries, and the old machinery doesn't reach the new terrain.  What they're describing now is actors who never touch classified information but can still do serious damage. Undisclosed lobbying. Covert campaign financing. Influence operations that run through civic organizations or think tanks or local media. None of that triggers the traditional tripwires. And that's not an accident.  So here's where I think this is heading.  **The convergence of OSINT exploitation and LinkedIn-style recruitment is going to get worse before any institution figures out how to slow it.**  The report flags professional networking platforms as a primary initial contact vector. This is already well documented in operational cases, the German BfV warned about it, MI5 put out a public advisory, the FBI has briefed on it repeatedly. But the scale is still underappreciated. LinkedIn has roughly a billion users. A foreign intelligence service running deceptive recruitment operations, posing as headhunters or academic collaborators or business development contacts, can run those operations at volume with minimal marginal cost. Each fake profile costs almost nothing to build. Each outreach message costs nothing to send. The targeting data is handed to them for free, publicly, by the targets themselves.  The tradecraft evolution to watch: these operations are going to get more granular in their targeting and more patient in their timelines. The early LinkedIn recruitment approaches were relatively blunt. "We have a consulting opportunity. Can we talk?" The more sophisticated version, which is already showing up in documented cases, involves sustained relationship-building over months, sometimes a year or more, before any ask materializes. The operator shows up as a credible peer. They engage with your publications. They offer something genuinely useful. They establish trust before the relationship becomes operational.  The implication for individuals is uncomfortable. If you have a detailed public professional profile, work in a policy-adjacent field, defense, energy, biotech, semiconductor supply chains, legislative staff, academia touching dual-use research, and you've never had a security briefing on what foreign intelligence recruitment actually looks like in practice, you are a plausible target and you probably don't know it. Not because you're paranoid or important, but because these services are running volume operations and they are very good at identifying who might be useful later.  **The multi-domain structure is the harder problem.**  The report documents simultaneous operations across political, economic, information, and security domains. The value of that structure isn't just breadth. It's that the operations in each domain feed the operations in the others. Intelligence collected about a political target's financial situation in the economic domain informs the recruitment pitch in the political domain. Disinformation seeded in the information domain shapes the public environment in ways that make certain political outcomes more achievable. Security domain collection, monitoring law enforcement and intelligence methodologies, lets the actor adapt and stay ahead of the defensive response.  Each piece, looked at individually, might seem manageable. The interaction effects are what makes it genuinely hard to disrupt. You can counter a disinformation campaign. You can identify and expel a foreign intelligence officer. But disrupting the whole system requires coordination across agencies, across sectors, and across governments that democratic systems are structurally not great at sustaining.  What this means for the near term: expect continued actor investment in the information domain, particularly in local and regional media ecosystems that have less capacity for scrutiny than national outlets, and in the academic sector, where the norm of open collaboration makes security culture hard to build without alienating the people you're trying to protect.  **The whole-of-society framing is correct but the implementation gap is enormous.**  Fivecast is right that private sector organizations, universities, and civil society groups are target sets without adequate defenses. But saying "we need whole-of-society awareness" is easier than building it. A few honest observations about why this is hard.  Universities are structurally resistant. International collaboration is core to how research works. Restricting it has real costs, not just procedurally but scientifically. The academics who most need security awareness are often the ones least culturally inclined to think in those terms, and telling a department head that their visiting scholar might be reporting to a foreign intelligence service is a conversation that tends to go badly.  Small and mid-sized companies in sensitive supply chains have basically no capacity. They don't have cleared personnel. They don't have security teams. They often don't even know they're in a sensitive supply chain until something goes wrong. The OSINT aggregation point in the report is particularly relevant here: a foreign actor doesn't need to compromise a prime contractor if they can build a detailed picture of a critical supplier's processes, personnel, and vulnerabilities from publicly available information.  Civil society is genuinely difficult. The organizations doing democracy promotion, election observation, diaspora community work, human rights documentation: they're targets and they operate in environments where security measures can compromise their effectiveness or their credibility. An election monitoring group that starts vetting its local partners for foreign intelligence connections is going to damage relationships it needs to do its job.  None of this means the whole-of-society approach is wrong. It means that "sustained investment in security awareness and institutional partnerships" is doing a lot of work in that conclusion, and the investment required is significantly larger and more sustained than any government has yet committed.  **What officials and legislators should actually be watching.**  The financial manipulation angle in the political domain deserves more attention than it typically gets. Undisclosed foreign financial support for political campaigns and advocacy organizations is harder to trace than a LinkedIn recruitment operation and potentially more consequential. The reporting requirements that exist in most democratic systems were designed for a slower, more visible form of influence. They're not keeping pace with the sophistication of the financial vehicles being used or the speed at which money can move through intermediary structures.  The counter-interference capacity to monitor these flows exists in fragments across financial intelligence units, campaign finance regulators, tax authorities, and counterintelligence agencies. The information sharing between them is inadequate in most jurisdictions. The legal authorities are patchwork. Closing those gaps requires legislative coordination that is slow even when there's political will to do it, which there often isn't because some of the money flowing through these structures benefits incumbents who write the laws.  If you're watching for leading indicators, look at whether financial intelligence authorities are being given expanded mandates and resources to track foreign beneficial ownership through complex corporate structures. Look at whether campaign finance disclosure requirements are being updated to cover new vehicle types. Look at whether there's meaningful information sharing between the entities that would need to cooperate to actually disrupt these flows. In most democracies right now, that coordination is not happening at the speed the threat requires.  **The adaptation problem.**  The security domain targeting note in the report is the one I'd want people to sit with the longest. Foreign actors systematically collect intelligence about law enforcement and intelligence agency operations, personnel, and methodologies specifically to monitor counter-interference efforts and adapt their tradecraft.  This is why the tradecraft evolution is continuous. The moment a detection method becomes known, the actor modifies the behavior being detected. The moment an expulsion or prosecution signals which methodology got burned, the actor retires that methodology and shifts to something the defender hasn't seen yet. It's not that defenders are incompetent. It's that they're operating against an adversary who is collecting intelligence specifically about their defensive methods.  The implication: public reporting on how specific operations were detected is a double-edged tool. It builds awareness. It also educates the adversary. There's no clean answer to that tension, but it's worth being clear-eyed that open-source practitioner assessments like this one are read by the people running the operations they describe.  The direction of travel here is toward higher-sophistication, lower-signature operations. Fewer operatives doing things that look like tradecraft from a spy novel. More patient, relationship-based, commercially-camouflaged activity that is genuinely hard to distinguish from legitimate business and academic engagement until well after the damage is done. The institutions built to catch the old version of this problem are going to keep finding that the threat has moved.  foreigninterference.org/post… #foreigninterference #ProfessionalNetworkRecruitment #DeceptiveJobRecruitment #AcademicEspionage #InfluenceOperations #CounterintelligenceTargeting #SocialMediaMonitoring #ThreatAssessmentFramework

The GEC's congressional moment has come and gone, and what the hearings actually documented is worth sitting with for a minute. Not the testimony about Russian active measures, though that was useful. The part where lawmakers and witnesses spent considerable time establishing that the agency tasked with coordinating the entire U.S. government's counter-disinformation response was, in practice, structurally underequipped to do it. Understaffed, underfunded, and operating in a bureaucratic environment where the State Department, DoD, and the intelligence community were all running their own parallel efforts without meaningful deconfliction.  That's the signal. Not that disinformation is bad and we should fight it. We knew that. The signal is that the U.S. counter-disinformation architecture has an institutional ceiling baked into its design, and adversaries who study us, and they do study us, can see that ceiling clearly.  So let's talk trajectory.  Russia's information operations did not become what they are through improvisation. The hearings walked through the historical lineage, Soviet active measures printing fabricated stories to set Black Americans against white Americans, young against old, recent immigrants against established communities. That methodology didn't retire when the USSR did. It evolved. The same strategic logic, exploit existing fractures rather than create new ones, got ported onto Facebook and Twitter and YouTube, scaled up by orders of magnitude, and aimed at the same fault lines with far more precision than a pamphlet operation could ever manage. The operational continuity between the KGB's Service A and the Internet Research Agency is not a metaphor. It's a documented inheritance.  What the hearings surface, and what should concern anyone watching the next phase, is that the adversary infrastructure producing this content is institutional. It has budgets, personnel pipelines, long-term planning cycles, and organizational memory. The GEC, by contrast, is fighting that with an interagency coordination mandate and a resource level that witnesses at both hearings described as inadequate to the scale of the problem.  The resource asymmetry testimony was not vague. Multiple witnesses made the same basic point: the investment adversary states put into disinformation operations vastly exceeds what the U.S. has committed to countering them. This isn't a gap you close with clever tactics. When one side has a structural resource advantage and the other side has a coordination problem on top of a resource problem, the tactical wins by the underfunded side tend to be temporary. You disrupt a campaign. The apparatus producing it runs the next one.  Here's what that means for the near term.  The GEC's mandate requires it to coordinate across the State Department, DoD, the intelligence community, and others. The hearings documented that this coordination has not been working smoothly. When you have parallel counter-disinformation efforts running without strategic alignment, a few things happen. Resources get duplicated in some areas and missed entirely in others. Attribution work by one agency doesn't flow cleanly to the public counter-narrative work another agency is trying to run. And adversaries who are watching, which again, they are, learn to target the seams. They push operations that fall between the jurisdictional boundaries. They time campaigns to exploit the lag between when something is detected and when a coordinated response can be authorized.  The legal constraint issue raised at the hearings deserves more attention than it usually gets, because it's not going away and it shapes everything downstream. Democratic governments can't just run domestic influence operations to counter foreign ones. The First Amendment parameters are real. This means the actual counter-disinformation toolkit for domestic audiences is heavily dependent on things the government doesn't fully control: platform content moderation, media literacy education, civil society organizations, journalism. Those are all valuable and genuinely important. They are also slow, inconsistent, and right now under significant political pressure from directions that have nothing to do with foreign interference.  Platform moderation, in particular, has shifted considerably since these hearings were held. The major platforms have reduced or restructured their trust and safety teams. Coordination between the U.S. government and platforms on foreign interference attribution has become politically fraught in ways it wasn't a few years ago. The legal and institutional environment that the GEC was designed to operate in has changed under its feet.  So if you're thinking about what comes next, think about that combination: a counter-disinformation coordinator with resource and coordination problems operating in a domestic information environment where the platform-level infrastructure it relied on has degraded, during an election cycle that adversaries have demonstrated interest in targeting across multiple consecutive cycles.  The European comparison raised at the hearings is instructive here, and not entirely in a comforting way. Countries like Finland, Estonia, and Sweden developed counter-disinformation resilience over decades of sustained Russian pressure. They built it into education systems, media ecosystems, and government communication practices as long-term investments. The United States has tried to build a comparable capacity in a fraction of the time, without the same sustained political commitment, in a domestic environment far more polarized and therefore far more exploitable. The European model works in part because the societies running it developed a shared understanding of the threat over time. That shared understanding is notably absent in the current U.S. political environment, and that absence is itself an operational advantage for the actors the GEC exists to counter.  The attribution challenge mentioned in the hearings also deserves a direct look. Definitively attributing disinformation operations to state sponsors in ways usable for public counter-narratives is hard. It requires technical evidence, legal review, and political authorization to declassify and release, all moving at different speeds. That lag between attribution and public disclosure is a window adversaries exploit. By the time a campaign is publicly attributed, its primary damage is often done. The narrative has spread, the division has been amplified, the targeted audience has already absorbed the content. The attribution announcement becomes a coda to an operation that already worked.  What to watch from here:  The GEC's budget and authority questions are not settled. Congressional interest in these hearings suggests scrutiny will continue, and the outcomes of that scrutiny will determine whether the coordination problems get fixed or institutionalized. Watch for whether the interagency deconfliction mechanisms get real resources and authority attached to them, or whether they stay as coordination frameworks on paper that each agency treats as optional.  Watch China. The hearings were heavily Russia-focused for good historical and analytical reasons, but the testimony acknowledged other adversaries explicitly. Chinese information operations have different objectives and different methods than Russian ones, they tend toward narrative shaping and long-term influence cultivation rather than acute disruption, but the scale and institutional depth of those operations is significant, and they have been accelerating. The GEC's Russia analysis is more mature. The China piece is catching up, but the institutional infrastructure for countering Chinese information operations in U.S. information spaces is less developed.  Watch what happens to the civil society and research organizations that have been doing significant attribution and analysis work outside government. Groups like the Stanford Internet Observatory, the Atlantic Council's Digital Forensic Research Lab, and others have been doing the kind of public-facing attribution work that the government can't always do quickly or openly. Several of these organizations have faced significant pressure in the last two years, including legal action and political targeting. Weakening that external research ecosystem doesn't just hurt academic knowledge production. It removes a significant part of the practical counter-disinformation infrastructure that the GEC's whole-of-government model implicitly depends on.  And watch, with particular attention, for operations targeting the legitimacy of the counter-disinformation effort itself. This is already happening and has been for a while. The framing of organizations like the GEC as censorship operations, the pressure on platform trust and safety teams as politically biased arbiters of speech, the litigation against researchers studying foreign influence: these are not random. Discrediting the people trying to document and counter foreign interference is a force multiplier for foreign interference. You don't have to run more operations if you can make the people exposing your operations politically toxic.  The GEC exists because the U.S. government decided state-sponsored disinformation was a first-order strategic threat. The hearings confirmed that assessment hasn't changed. What the hearings also showed is that the gap between that threat assessment and the operational capacity to address it remains wide, and the political environment required to close that gap has become more complicated, not less, since the agency was established.  That's the trajectory. The threat is institutional and it is patient. The response is underfunded, under-coordinated, and operating in a domestic environment that has become harder to work in. That is a combination that tends not to improve on its own.  foreigninterference.org/post… #foreigninterference #CounterDisinformationFrameworkDevelopment #DisinformationCampaigns #ComputationalPropaganda #DemocraticInstitutionTargeting #StateMediaCoordination #InfluenceOperations

"The Information Speed ​​Difference"  The most dangerous aspect of this structure isn't misinformation or false reports. It's the "speed difference" of the information itself. Even if the truth exists, if it arrives too slowly, reality will have already changed.  As usual, multiple update reports lined the conference room this morning. But this time, the frequency of updates was the problem. Some information was updated every few minutes, while others arrived hours or even days late. This asynchronous nature was distorting decision-making.  I saw a structural disconnect there.  They were dealing with the same reality, yet the "present" they were referencing was different.  At the morning briefing, the intelligence analyst said:  "This isn't a war; it's an update race."  Those words weren't an exaggeration. Rather, they were almost too accurate. Increasingly, how military actions are reported, interpreted, and disseminated has more influence than the military actions themselves.  In other words, the initiative in reality is shifting from action to updates.  Before noon, reports came in about small-scale clashes in southern Lebanon. However, the details differed subtly across multiple sources. The time, scale, and those involved—none of them matched. But no one could immediately determine "which is correct."  The important point here isn't the question of truth or falsity.  It's which version will take hold first.  In the afternoon, a short report from the diplomatic team. The US statement and the Israeli statement described the same event in different contexts. The US called it "local tension management," while Israel called it "continuous threat response."  The same event, yet completely separate in its meaning.  I see a crucial point there.  The information is shared, but the meaning isn't.  In the evening, the energy market manager quietly remarked:  "The market isn't trading the news, it's trading the delay in the news."  This statement hit the nail on the head. The market isn't betting on reality itself, but on the time lag before the interpretation of reality stabilizes.  In other words, uncertainty is a matter of time, not content.  At night, additional information arrives. A statement rapidly spreads on social media, and within hours, it is officially denied. However, the denial does not stop the spread. Rather, the denial itself is re-spread with a different meaning.  This cycle is already out of control.  I wrote in my notebook:  "Information is not fact; it functions as a delay structure."  At that moment, the core of this chapter becomes clear.  In this world, managing the speed difference of information is more important than its accuracy. It's not about who knows first, but who "fixes the meaning first."  Outside the window, night was deepening. The city lights seemed constant, but behind them, countless updates were running simultaneously. Silence is merely a state where updates are in equilibrium.  Chapter 8 stood on that equilibrium.
